Interview Transcript
Jamare Bates  |  JLL
Vice President, Clean Energy & Infrastructure Advisory
Conducted by Clayton Hester & Victor Pan  |  April 2026  |  Live Call  |  Lightly edited for readability
JAMARE: It’s not too bad of a day here in Texas. What part of Texas? Is it Texas A&M?
CLAYTON: Yes, sir. College Station. Right in the middle of what they’re calling the Golden Triangle — Austin, Houston, and Dallas.
JAMARE: College Station. Yes, sir. I lived near Fort Hood, Copperas Cove, Waco — maybe an hour or so from Austin. Very familiar. Good afternoon, Victor. This is Jamare Bates. How are you both feeling?
VICTOR: Doing great, thank you.
CLAYTON: Yes, sir. So we’re doing some research in the energy industry for an MBA project. The whole project is centered around putting us in front of an industry we’ve never really been a part of — to learn about something we might not have taken the time to explore before. Our entire project is centered around energy management — how it’s done, what the holes and problems are, and whether the solutions some startups are building are ones that actually matter to the customers they’re targeting.
JAMARE: That’s good. So do you want to just ask me questions, do you want me to give you some of my background, talk about the industry in general, or maybe all of the above?
VICTOR: Could you please give all three? We’d love a personalized background that goes beyond your LinkedIn, and then we have some curated questions to follow up with.
JAMARE: Do you mind if we do a transcription on this?
VICTOR: Oh please, yes. We’d really love that.
JAMARE: So again, Jamare Bates, Vice President at JLL. I’m in our Energy Advisory group. Our energy advisory group is effectively a small team within the consulting side of JLL. JLL has property management, facility management, leasing and brokerage, and then a consulting side. Our main goal is to bring expertise in renewable energy, electric vehicles, battery energy storage, and other energy efficiency and sustainability infrastructure that our commercial real estate clients are looking for.
JAMARE: I’ve been with JLL for about three years, and I’ve got about — well, it’s 2026 now — almost 25 years of experience in engineering and construction programs and project management. The early part of my career was primarily focused on horizontal construction — roads, bridges, highways, infrastructure. Then in the middle part of my career I did a lot of work for the Department of the Army and Department of the Navy in critical facility upgrades and programs at military installations. Now our team is more focused on commercial clients, with private sector clients as our primary target.
JAMARE: Public sector clients — including state and local governments — are part of our base, but the changes in administration are having a big impact at the federal level and trickling down to state and local governments, because a lot of them are getting federal funding to support their energy efficiency and sustainability initiatives. We can talk more about that if you have specific questions.
JAMARE: One of the primary areas I’m focused on is energy savings — helping clients identify what they can do to reduce energy costs or retrofit their facilities to lower consumption. The other area is electric vehicle fleet conversion. Many of our private sector clients are moving forward with EV fleet transitions regardless of federal tax credits. And then, at a macro level, energy is at the very forefront of everybody’s talking points right now.
JAMARE: In the last year or two — going back to 2022 and 2023 — the main focus was data centers and AI, and that is still an incredibly important macroeconomic driver. Because of the Middle Eastern conflicts happening right now, it’s not changing people’s thoughts about energy so much as it’s accelerating what some of our clients were already trying to do. Much of what we were talking about two and three years ago in terms of risks to the energy markets is being realized in a big way. We’ve got clients flooding in saying: you’ve been telling us these things were critical, and our energy costs are now going through the roof. How do we handle that?
JAMARE: From a macroeconomic perspective, global energy is going to become the most important and most critical factor going forward. In a commercial real estate sense, we used to joke that when a new entity was trying to find their headquarters or their biggest factory, the number one question was, where’s the cheapest land and the best workforce? Now the number one question is: where is the cheapest energy? The workforce will come to me. People will move for jobs, much more than they used to.
VICTOR: How does JLL fit into the energy scene, and how are you helping companies save money on their energy bills?
JAMARE: Great question, and very timely. If I give you the history of my team — Energy Advisory is a small team because it’s a new team. New in the six-to-eight-year sense. The reason it was stood up is that in the 2016 to 2018 timeframe, Tesla was becoming big, electric vehicles were becoming a real market, and commercial real estate clients were starting to ask questions — I own this building, I have a big parking lot, what should I do with EVs? At the time, JLL didn’t have professionals internally with that expertise. So we stood up a team and built that service out.
JAMARE: Every piece of real estate requires energy. It’s the center point of basically everything. So from a business perspective, we could refer clients to specialty firms that do EV or solar, or we could bring that expertise in-house and provide it directly to our clients. We chose to bring it in-house. That’s really how JLL fits into the energy space.
VICTOR: What are the main problems you’re seeing in the industry right now when it comes to energy management?
JAMARE: The biggest problem right now is that we are at an evolutionary inflection point. We lived through a long time when energy was cheap and demand grew at a slow, predictable rate. When you had that environment and were trying to bring in new technologies — renewables, wind, storage — those deals didn’t always pencil out for the return on investment our clients were looking for. That’s why the federal government came in with tax incentives and rebates, to make those deals work and accelerate adoption.
JAMARE: Because of data centers and AI, demand for energy is now spiking exponentially compared to historical norms. The biggest challenge is that most of our clients were making decisions based on short-term returns. A few years back they were saying, this return doesn’t make sense to us, we’re not going to do this deal. We were saying, but you don’t understand the long-term trend, you need to do it while you have the capital. Now they’re coming back saying, we need to do this now, and we’re saying, it’s going to take five years to stand up.
JAMARE: The biggest challenge is energy infrastructure takes significant capital. And then on the efficiency side, we have antiquated facilities. The U.S. real estate market has traditionally operated under a use-existing-and-retrofit model, rather than building new. Buildings were built when energy was cheap, so energy efficiency was never a primary design constraint. A building that was 70% efficient was acceptable when energy costs were low. Now that energy costs have escalated sharply, that inefficiency has become a major financial liability.
JAMARE: Add in the office vacancy crisis: nobody wants to go to work unless the office is Class A — glass walls, sunlight, plants, a gym and daycare. If you’re in a low-tier office park, your occupancy is suffering. Developers sitting on buildings that are 35% occupied are losing money. Can they retrofit the building into condos? The conversion often doesn’t pencil out either. So there’s this friction point: do we repurpose it, or do we put the capital in now that we should have been putting in for the last decade?
VICTOR: And those older buildings are also costing companies a lot in energy.
JAMARE: Right. Let me use random numbers — say a building is 70% energy efficient. When energy is cheap, that’s not your biggest expense. Your biggest expense is payroll. But now that energy costs have escalated at a faster rate than returns from that asset, you’re stuck. You’re in a seven-year lease — what are you supposed to do? You can either ride it out or invite us in to help negotiate with the landlord and get to a position where you can mitigate the delta.
VICTOR: I’m curious about your thoughts on this AI startup we’ve been working with as part of our project. It’s a research call, not a sales call. The product is centered on using AI to sift through the tens of thousands of documents that come in every month for large multi-location companies. A lot of energy management teams are sinking through these bills manually, using OCR to pull data points into spreadsheets and then doing the math. This product takes all those documents, sifts through them with AI, and lets you chat with the data to find energy savings and discrepancies. They’ve found millions of dollars in savings — sometimes hundreds of thousands — because companies are overpaying or have demand spikes from a solar panel that triggered a peak demand charge for the entire year without them knowing. Is this the kind of tool that JLL would see as an asset to sell to clients?
JAMARE: Great question. Let me give some context first. JLL is a global company, over 100,000 employees, usually top one, two, or three real estate firm in the world. We deal with the full spectrum of the market, typically Fortune 200 but pretty much all of the Fortune 500 level companies.
JAMARE: To your point, we have an internal team that builds things like that. We even have investment in the market into firms like that. We are a very pro-AI company in terms of use and proficiency. But you’re going to have different market tiers. The biggest firms have enough to build things in-house. That’s enterprise level. And sometimes a company like the one you’re describing might have something worthwhile, but with a firm like JLL, the enterprise-level discussion usually leads to acquisition — because we’re not going to continue paying for something we can just buy.
JAMARE: The next tier down — your mid-cap companies — they’re in a position where they have to decide whether to build in-house or buy from the market. That’s where a software solution like this would be most valuable. And then you’ve got the low end of the market, where they have very small projects and the nominal difference between using the tool versus doing it by hand is so small the value proposition doesn’t hold. You always have different market tiers.
VICTOR: They’re targeting more like Home Depots and CVS — companies with many locations and a lot of revenue.
JAMARE: Okay, so mid-market but maybe higher end too. That profile makes sense. I can’t speak to the specific product, but I will say the biggest challenge for a company like that is getting access to clients’ data. They’re going to have to figure out a model that gets them data access in a way that appears valuable to the client, because many of these higher-end clients do not want a third party knowing how much they’re spending on power. They don’t want that information in the market. And they’re going to be worried about whether an AI company is training models on their data and potentially sharing it with others.
JAMARE: So the number one thing I would tell you is: make sure the company is thinking about how they’re going to manage and keep the data secure, and maybe even anonymize it. If a Fortune 200 company finds out that a thousand of their invoices for power over the last five years got out on the internet, that company is going to be crushed.
VICTOR: With the tools JLL is building internally, have you found positive results?
JAMARE: Yes. One of the benefits of JLL having a large footprint is that we have a lot of data. We manage data for our clients, so we already have it. For us to build tools trained on data we already know and can be tailored to what we know to look for — we can train it to find specific things. As a company, I don’t even know the exact number, but we’ve found millions and millions and millions of dollars of savings for our clients. And it’s not just one AI tool — it’s a suite. This tool does one thing, this tool is human-based, this one is AI-based. There’s a variety.
VICTOR: That must have been a big project to stand up.
JAMARE: We have a whole division — JLL Technologies — within the company. We also have property management software, facility management software, building automation system integrations. If you know anything about how buildings are operated, they have BAS systems — building automation systems — that monitor temperature, manage thermostats, flag when Pump 10 is broken or a variable air valve isn’t working properly. There’s inherent technology infrastructure within commercial real estate that JLL can leverage when building these tools.
VICTOR: This interview has been amazing. We’ve gotten a lot of great insights from you. Are there other people you’d recommend we talk to about this subject?
JAMARE: Let me think about that. Maybe not specific to JLL, but I’ll give you a target to consider. You mentioned energy savings. Some of what you’re talking about, energy auditors do this work by hand right now. If I were you, I would want to speak to current energy auditors. Two reasons. One — if this tool is built to take their job, they’re going to have a feeling about it. If it’s built to make their job easier, now you might have a target market. People’s fear of AI is that it’s going to take their job. But while you’re afraid that AI is going to take your job, somebody else is learning how to do your job with AI — and that’s the person who’s going to take your job.
VICTOR: Thank you so much. That’s a really great point.
JAMARE: I’ve got a question for you both. You’re both in an MBA program. When you look out into the job market, what are a couple of things you’re worried about, and what are you excited about?
VICTOR: My undergrad was computer science, so the scary thing is that AI can now do a lot of that coding. You don’t even need to know programming to do a lot of things. That’s a little scary, but it’s also exciting because the baseline knowledge required is lower now. I can combine what I already know with these new tools and go further. It does make me worried about entry-level positions disappearing, though.
JAMARE: That’s a good answer. Same question for you, Clayton.
CLAYTON: My worry, as someone who wants to be a business owner one day — someone who’s very entrepreneurial — is that the solutions that are going to come up, I won’t be part of the ones creating them. At a bigger level, I worry about how humanity is going to adapt to an industry changing this fast, one that’s building upon itself exponentially. People like us on this call are going to adapt to it really well and be excited about it. But so many people out there are worried about AI taking their jobs — about not being able to live and eat. My excitement, though, is the opposite of that fear. When autopilot came out for planes, it didn’t reduce the number of pilots — it slingshot the industry. More people became pilots because the barrier to entry came down. I think the same thing is going to happen here. The jobs that people hate doing — the ones where people are miserable — will be automated. And they’ll be able to find work that actually gives them fulfillment.
JAMARE: Both really good answers. From an employer standpoint, what’s valued is critical thinking. Victor, having foundational computer science knowledge means you can write better prompts than most people, because you understand how a computer actually works. When I was coming up I had to take C++. I don’t code anymore, but when I interact with AI, I write prompts almost like computer code — structured, logical. That background gets you 80% of the way through the minutia you don’t want to deal with, and lets you use your brain for the high-level critical thinking that turns AI-generated output into something real.
JAMARE: For you, Clayton — that’s an important one. Technology doesn’t make a net loss in jobs. It changes jobs completely. I’m old enough to remember pagers. What happened to all the pager manufacturers? Nobody has a job making pagers anymore. But they started making smartphones. And the people who lost pager jobs — the increase in jobs making apps, games, streaming content — none of that existed because the internet didn’t exist when we had pagers. AI is doing the same thing. We’re at the big gray brick cell phone stage of AI. When it becomes the underlying layer of everything, the people who understand it will be able to build on top of it. The companies and problems that need entrepreneurial solutions — they haven’t been invented yet.
JAMARE: I’ve got lights on my screen so I look good on video calls. They didn’t need to make those before COVID, because people didn’t have video calls that much. Now there’s a whole industry of products for video and camera shooting. None of that existed. Same thing with AI. For people afraid of what AI will take away — have a mindset that it’s going to be net new, and you’re always looking for the new opportunities.
VICTOR: I really appreciate that. That’s a great way to look at it.
JAMARE: Stay connected. If you’re on LinkedIn, connect with me and follow me. And we’re still a growing company — shoot me your resume if you want.
CLAYTON: Thank you so much, we really appreciate your time.
JAMARE: Glad I could help. Talk soon. Take care.

And so our primary goal is to make sure we maintain temperature and humidity within critical spaces. So we came up with a way to do that that's pretty effective with controlling a controlling with controlling a control valve. So
We look at, we look at the enthalpy in the space, and then we'll control a coin coil control valve to change the discharge temperature sensor, not the changes the sensor, but it changes the set point based on space conditions. So, it's constantly thinking things. It works kind of like AI.
It's continuously modulating based on daytime, nighttime, no matter what type of temperature it is, to maintain the space, the two space condition points that we want. So we want a certain temperature and we want a certain humidity. And looking at the enthalpy of that, we can modulate the discharge air temperature.
And then at that point, we don't care what the season is outside. And it just works. It works around the clock. And what it does is it helps keep our humidity levels higher in the winter, which helps when it comes to compliance, but it also helps keep our humidity levels low within range in the summer when it's really hot and humid.

═══ SOURCE: CC_Research_Don_Johnson_Trane_Transcript.docx | FOLDER: Call Transcripts ═══
Interview Transcript
Don Johnson | Trane Technologies
CEM, Senior Account Manager — El Paso, TX
Conducted by Clayton Hester & Victor Pan  |  March 2026
Participant Profile
Transcript
Clayton Hester
Hi, how you doing?
Don Johnson
Well, afternoon, Clayton.
Clayton Hester
How's your day been?
Don Johnson
Busy and good. We're doing some energy RFP work right now. And we had an ice cream party today so that's always fun.
Clayton Hester
Ice cream party — what's your favorite flavor?
Don Johnson
This was from a boutique company called Jeni's. I tried the ooey gooey cake something or other. No notes. It was good.
Clayton Hester
So, walk us through what your job role is and what Trane does for clients.
Don Johnson
I'm currently an account manager. We have different types — systems account managers who primarily deal with engineers and contractors, and then there's me: I work directly with customers. I provide solutions. Energy management type solutions, energy savings, sustainability. A lot of people are moving towards electrification so we help them meet their sustainability and energy efficiency goals. We also sell service agreements and do turnkey projects. A lot of it is bundling — air conditioning solutions with energy solutions, or anything else they need in that facility.
Clayton Hester
Who are some of your key clients?
Don Johnson
I started at Trane about two years ago, so as a new account manager I kind of got the scraps. A lot of the customers I brought on are in New Mexico: Western New Mexico University, New Mexico State University, Las Cruces Public Schools, the City of Las Cruces. Here in Texas I handle UTEP, the University of Texas El Paso. Some industrial customers too.
Clayton Hester
When you meet with these customers, what are they really looking for out of a relationship with Trane?
Don Johnson
First, they want a company they can rely on that's going to be around. Trane's been around 113 years so that gives them reassurance. But they also want a company that can do more than one thing — not just equipment, but stand behind it and provide maintenance throughout its life expectancy. And as a controls company, they're looking at how we can help them be more efficient and optimize how they're utilizing their buildings. The big thing right now is smart buildings. Everyone's talking AI. Trane purchased BrainBox AI, which can implement AI solutions with any controls, not just Trane controls. So we can offer AI building optimization even to customers who don't have our BAS.
Clayton Hester
Is savings a big conversation point, or is it mainly solving issues they're facing?
Don Johnson
Most customers are still reactive on the maintenance side. I run into a lot who have deferred maintenance — they don't have the manpower or the budget to do really good maintenance. So a lot of times we're helping them bring their equipment back up to where it should be running. They don't call us until they need to. Even customers you wouldn't think operated like that — like hospitals — they do.
Clayton Hester
Do you ever look at their energy bills?
Don Johnson
Yes. I'm a certified energy manager with the Association of Energy Engineers. One of the things I used to do at El Paso Electric was a lot of energy analysis — profiling customer energy use, meeting with them periodically, and offering solutions or ideas to reduce their energy use. Which is kind of counterproductive for an electric company, right? But that's what we did. I still do that here at Trane, though I don't have direct access to their historical utility data like I used to. I have to get their permission now. It can be time intensive, so it depends on how my day goes.
Clayton Hester
So the research we're doing is with an energy management company called Clear Current. It's an AI software platform that connects utility and building data, reads every line item across every site, flags errors and savings opportunities, and lets anyone on the team ask questions — kind of like a ChatGPT for your energy bill. You drop the documents in and it spots opportunities, anomalies, organizes invoices. When you hear about something like that, what comes to mind?
Don Johnson
One thing I'd say is — are they looking at power factor? Because all utilities now are penalizing for low power factor and it can be pretty big charges. Power factor correction is usually a quick return on investment, within a couple years with capacitor banks. That's something they should flag.
Clayton Hester
When you were doing energy bill work at El Paso Electric, what surprised you about how companies behaved toward their bills? Did they dive deep or just pay it?
Don Johnson
A lot of them just don't understand even the rate they're on. Those tiered rates are pretty complicated. Utilities are trying to move everyone to time-of-use rates, and the peak profile is shifting now with electric vehicles charging at night. Peak is now 4 or 5 p.m. where it used to be 2 p.m. So customers on old rates could potentially be on a better structure if someone analyzed their actual usage pattern.

I also saw customers at the pivot point of their demand threshold. Here in Texas, if you're peaking at 600 kW too many times consecutively, you get moved to the large power service rate, and demand charges are a lot higher. And then there are ratchets — if your highest peak during summer months is 1,000 kW, your minimum demand charge for the next 11 months is 75% of that, so 750 kW, even if you only use 500. That's where controls come in. You can do demand limiting to keep customers below that threshold and save them thousands of dollars.
Clayton Hester
If you could use something like Clear Current and chat with the energy bill — how would you want to interact with it? What would you ask?
Don Johnson
What would be great is if I could enter all the customer data and ask it: generate me an energy profile for this customer, for whatever time period I want. Or pinpoint when they had their highest peak demand. What month were they using the most kilowatt hours? What was usage during peak times versus off-peak? Things like that. It takes a very long time to put that analysis together manually. Anything to save me time and help me help a customer so I can move on to the next job — I'm all for it.
Clayton Hester
We've been talking about the difference between an energy expert user versus a CFO who just wants to know how to save money. Where do you fall?
Don Johnson
I think it would be useful for both. Even someone who's just a layman would benefit from being able to get almost instant information — as long as the data is accurate. I could even see it being useful for customers considering solar. You could determine how they're using energy during the times when solar would be most productive, and calculate the cost avoidance and payback of a solar installation. Great tool for that kind of analysis.
Clayton Hester
Is there anything else that comes to mind when you hear about Clear Current — any other points from your experience working with companies that have many locations?
Don Johnson
What's the name of the product again?
Clayton Hester
Clear Current Technologies.
Don Johnson
Are they focused on big customers or are they also looking at small commercial? Because small commercial customers are really the ones who don't have the support. They're not going to have an energy manager. Just a general manager with a five-year business plan.
Clayton Hester
Right now they're focused on large enterprises — multiple locations, high energy consumption. But their goal is to eventually serve smaller companies with a more layman-friendly interface too.
Don Johnson
Makes sense. When I was at the electric company, the large organizations were already using third-party energy management companies to oversee and analyze their energy usage. Those companies would monitor their rates, how they were using energy, and contact us if they saw something. But I don't know if any of them had a tool like this — using AI to automatically deliver insights on usage. That's different.

I'll say this: it seems like Clear Current and companies like it are just going to keep popping up everywhere. I mean, these major softwares that everybody's been operating off of for so long are being replaced by something a 22-year-old built. I don't know who'll be around in 5 years. Probably multiple players with different market share. But it's an exciting time.
Clayton Hester
Any thoughts on dashboards or how the information should be presented?
Don Johnson
Dashboards are important — putting all the information in one place that's easy to read and understand. Not just for the energy expert, but for the energy expert to explain it to the end user. So they can understand it without flipping through a bunch of pages and being overwhelmed. If someone like me could go to a customer with better knowledge about their own usage, I can come up with quicker solutions than starting from scratch. That'd be a very useful tool.
Clayton Hester
Any connections you'd recommend we talk to?
Don Johnson
If any of your targets are public entities like school districts, have you heard of the Texas Energy Managers Association — TEMA? They have chapters throughout the state and all members work in energy or manage energy. Mostly energy managers at school districts, universities, community colleges. The Association of Energy Engineers also has a lot of chapters. Those are good starting points.
Clayton Hester
Thank you so much, Don. Really appreciate your time and all the insight.
Don Johnson
Good talking to you, Clayton. Sounds like a great product. Good luck with it.
Prepared by Clayton Hester  |  CDL MBA Consulting Team
Clear Current Technologies  |  Dan Schreiber (CPO) & John Reuter (CEO)

Interview Transcript
Texas State University | Utilities & Energy Management
Andee Chamberlain, Carl Teague, James Norton
Conducted by Clayton Hester  |  March 2026
Participant Profiles
Transcript
Clayton Hester
Hi, how you doing?
Carl Teague
Hello, doing well. How are you today?
Clayton Hester
I'm doing great. Thank you so much for taking this call.
Carl Teague
No problem. So you're with Texas State, correct?
Clayton Hester
Yes. Andee should be joining us shortly.
Clayton Hester
I'm an MBA student at A&M University with the Mays Business School. I got my undergrad in management in 2025 and I'm currently getting my MBA, focusing on entrepreneurial leadership. This project has us working with a startup company helping them collect information on how they can make their product better. The product is in energy management — specifically how AI is affecting how large enterprises manage energy. So the questions I have are really around how y'all operate and what your year looks like from an energy management perspective.
Andee Chamberlain
Hi Clayton, sorry I'm a little bit late. I was typing up an email. Go ahead.
Clayton Hester
No worries. I was just introducing the project — we're working with a startup focused on energy management and trying to understand how large corporations manage energy throughout the year. One thing they really want to understand is what the calendar year looks like for someone managing energy at a large institution. Like what does a year look like?
Andee Chamberlain
Yeah, I mean, it's a little different for us. We buy our electricity from the City of San Marcos — it's a municipal utility so we don't really have an option. We're not in a deregulated area. In deregulated areas you have more options and typically do a 3-to-5-year purchase. We can't do that for electricity, but we do purchase our natural gas. That's where we decide: do we go with the market rate or buy strips — a portion of our load at a set price? We negotiate that with an energy broker. Those decisions are typically done on a 3-year term, not annual.
Clayton Hester
Are there certain months throughout the year where your head's down on budgeting or procurement?
Andee Chamberlain
Yeah, in January and February, administration is getting together asking what we want to request for the next fiscal year budget. Our fiscal year runs September 1 to August 31. So we start planning early in the calendar year, those requests go through, get approved or not, and then budget moves forward for the next FY. But also happening is planning 3, 4, 5 years in advance. Carl, do you want to weigh in?
Carl Teague
Yeah. We'll get a team together and use our utility analyst. They give us the numbers and we'll go through 3 to 5 years of historical data and look at trends. Then we factor in what we know is coming — new buildings coming online, old buildings coming down, and how that affects our demand. We try to allot money for those new demands. We almost never see decreases anymore. It's always adding about 5 to 8 percent every year recently.
Clayton Hester
Is that 8% more energy or more in terms of cost?
Carl Teague
Cost. The energy use might actually go down if we demo an older building, because older buildings are bigger energy hogs even if the replacement is larger. But it's always the cost increases that are the issue.
Andee Chamberlain
And water is also becoming a utility we have to worry about more now. We have well water but with drought restrictions we're bumping up against our allocations, and the cost has increased quite a bit.
Clayton Hester
Are there any high-stress moments throughout the year when it comes to energy, or is it pretty plan-heavy in the front and then you just ride it out?
Andee Chamberlain
It's stressful when something like Winter Storm Uri happens, or even a week of really cold weather, or global events like the war that change the gas markets. If we haven't purchased ahead of time on the natural gas side, and we're buying on the market, it gets scary. The electricity is more set — we kind of have to pay what we pay. But natural gas on the market is stressful if you haven't insulated yourself by buying strips.
James Norton
And that's just on the purchasing side. On the service side — Carl and I are definitely biting our nails during those events. We've got emergency generators on campus and we have to run them under load. We budgeted $3.50 per gallon for diesel and we just went through a period where it was $5 a gallon. We did not forecast for that. Money has to come from somewhere else to cover it.
Clayton Hester
How do y'all manage your data? Is it manual, or do you use software?
Andee Chamberlain
We have a data analyst who keeps track of all of our usage. All billing for utilities gets entered and uploaded into EnergyCAP — the system we use. A lot of universities use that. It allows billing information to come in alongside our usage data and that's what we use to create projections and store historical data.
Clayton Hester
Have you ever found savings in your bills or is it usually more a matter of managing costs going up?
Andee Chamberlain
It's more often the latter, but yes. Even finding bills that shouldn't come to us — why are we paying this? This should be charged to someone else. With 1,500 meters, billing can be hard to track. It's not just our situation — everybody with multiple facilities and multiple meters deals with this. We had a weird multiplier happen on one of our wastewater bills. That's why you keep auditing your bills. If you're typically spending 3,500 gallons a month and now it's 35,000, something's wrong. The software will throw flags but then you have to dig into it manually.
Clayton Hester
So — and this isn't a sales call, just research — the company we're working with is building an AI energy management tool. You upload your documents or sync them with your provider and you can chat with your energy bill like it's ChatGPT. It spots errors on invoices, identifies savings opportunities, works as a tariff and contract analyzer. When you hear that, does it sound like a tool you'd use?
Andee Chamberlain
It sounds kind of like what we already have with EnergyCAP, but we don't use the bill processing module right now. We had someone doing our bill processing for 10 to 15 years and she recently left, so we're scrambling a little. We'll probably outsource that. So yeah, it's a service I think we'd benefit from.
Clayton Hester
Is there anything EnergyCAP can't do that you wish it could?
Andee Chamberlain
We have our own metering for the district energy system — we generate steam and chilled water at central plants and meter each building that receives them. That metering data is different from the utility meter we have from the City of San Marcos. Blending all of that into one system has been challenging. EnergyCAP tells us they can do that, but it's harder than it sounds. I see potential for AI to help with that — watching the data and creating internal billing from it.
Clayton Hester
If you had a chat interface with all your documents right there — every energy bill, every piece of the puzzle — what questions would you ask it?
Andee Chamberlain
Who's my biggest user? What's the EUI — energy utilization index — per square foot or per person in each building? That tells us where to focus conservation efforts. Where's our biggest bang for our buck?
Carl Teague
Possibly looking at events that are off-hours. If there's a university event at our event center on a Saturday, how did that impact our consumption compared to a normal school day? If I knew the event schedule and estimated attendance in advance, I could prepare for it. If that event happens every single year, I could prepare even better.
James Norton
Anomalies. If we're typically using X amount of energy at a certain building and it suddenly increases — that's the biggest thing we'd want to know about right away. It could mean a leak, or the space is being used differently. You want to know immediately.
Clayton Hester
Any other thoughts on what would make the product really useful?
James Norton
Cost recovery. Right now we charge buildings for utilities based on square footage. If we had better information about actual usage per building, we could charge based on actual consumption — and that would drive conservation. It's hard to encourage efficiency when you're charging people based on square footage regardless of what they actually use.
Clayton Hester
How many locations does Texas State manage with their own energy?
Carl Teague
Approximately 98 buildings. The ones connected to our district energy system get one big meter at the plant level. The City of San Marcos serves some buildings directly and those get individual bills. And then we have wastewater billed separately off water consumption. We're kind of a hodgepodge — it depends on where the building is on campus.
Clayton Hester
Have any of you personally gone through a utility bill yourselves?
Andee Chamberlain
Yes, but not regularly. It's really complex. I'm grateful I don't have to do it monthly. Things change, and our system for gathering that data and entering it into our database is also a little wonky. One of my issues is that I care more about the demand data and usage information, but the bill processing folks care more about the bottom line numbers — how much did it cost last month? We're competing about what kind of data we're extracting from it.
Clayton Hester
Is there anything else unique to share that would help this company understand how to build their product?
Andee Chamberlain
Metering is really hard, especially in district energy systems like most universities and hospitals have. Sub-metering all of that is complex and underserved. And meters and their data drop off — it's not like you install a meter and program it and it's always perfect. Operations change, maybe it wasn't installed correctly, maybe the data feed fails. It's a constant effort just to keep up with whether the data is right. I see big potential for AI to help there.
Andee Chamberlain
Also — large corporations with a chiller in their own building and a couple of meters coming in have a very different situation from us. We're generating at the plant and distributing across campus. The way we gather data is fundamentally different, and a platform that can handle both models would be valuable.
Clayton Hester
What does the year look like quarter to quarter?
Andee Chamberlain
We monitor and report monthly — past usage, projected costs and use — and management sees that report every month. But in terms of purchasing or planning, that's done on a fiscal year basis. Pre-fiscal year we do the budget allocation, and then it trues up over time and money gets moved around as needed. The big stuff — capital projects, major purchases — is more on a 3-to-5-year term.
Clayton Hester
Thank you all so much. Really appreciate everyone being on this call. If you have any questions or connections for us, please email them over. And I'd love to share the research with you when it's done if the company allows it.
Andee Chamberlain
We'd love to see it. Thank you, Clayton.
Carl Teague
Thanks. Good luck.
James Norton
Yeah, great talking to you. Take care.
Prepared by Clayton Hester  |  CDL MBA Consulting Team
Clear Current Technologies  |  Dan Schreiber (CPO) & John Reuter (CEO)

═══ SOURCE: Clear_Current_Interview_Summary_Adam_Zavadsky_ENGIE_Impact (1).docx | FOLDER: Interview Summary Documents ═══
Interview Summary
Adam Zavadsky | ENGIE Impact
Senior Account Manager
Conducted by Clayton Hester  |  April 14, 2026
1. Interviewee Profile
Adam's path to energy management was unconventional. He came out of investment banking — where he spent roughly 10 years — and transitioned to energy consulting through a college friend who worked in the field. He joined what was then an independent energy consulting firm that was later acquired by ENGIE's broader family, and has been building client relationships in the outsourced utility management space ever since. His finance background gives him a lens on the business that differs from the engineering-trained energy managers who dominate most of the organizations Clear Current is targeting.
2. ENGIE Impact — Business Model & Operations
2.1 Core Value Proposition: Outsourced Payables + Centralized Data
ENGIE Impact's foundational offering is outsourced utility bill processing. For clients like Panda Restaurant Group, all utility and waste invoices — across thousands of sites — are routed to ENGIE Impact, where they are received, reviewed, entered into the system, and paid on the client's behalf in a daily consolidated payment run. Adam described this as a 'labor avoidance' function that also creates the data infrastructure for everything else ENGIE does.
Implication for Clear Current: ENGIE Impact's competitive moat is not the dashboard or the analytics — it's the data. They have 20 years of Panda's utility data in their system. A new AI-native platform entering this relationship would need to either partner with ENGIE Impact or displace them entirely to access that data. This is the structural access problem Clear Current must solve in the QSR vertical.
2.2 Service Stack on Top of Bill Processing
Once centralized bill data exists, ENGIE Impact layers additional services on top of it: energy procurement consulting, waste management optimization, recycling and diversion consulting, sustainability reporting, and scope 1/2/3 emissions inventories. Clients can subscribe to any combination of these. For Panda, ENGIE Impact manages both utility bill processing and waste consulting, but Panda handles proactive energy management programs in-house.
Implication for Clear Current: ENGIE Impact is a full-service energy and sustainability firm, not a software product. The competitive framing for Clear Current is not head-to-head software vs. software — it is a targeted AI intelligence layer that fills the analytical gap between ENGIE's bill processing function and Panda's internal decision-making.
3. Key Interview Findings
3.1 AI at ENGIE Impact: Internally Focused, Not Client-Facing
When asked about AI adoption, Adam drew a clear distinction: ENGIE Impact is using AI primarily to improve internal bill processing workflows, not to enhance client-facing analytics.
Adam identified the core data intake challenge: large utilities like Pacific Gas & Electric have electronic data feeds that flow directly into ENGIE's system. But the roughly 30,000 municipalities in the United States — which bill for water and sewer — often have no digital infrastructure at all. ENGIE still receives paper bills from many of these, scans them with a large-volume scanner, runs OCR, and then has data entry staff manually key the numbers in because there is no consistent format.
Implication for Clear Current: The bill format problem that Phil Combs identified from the Trane side is confirmed here from the ENGIE side. Paper bills from small municipalities are the persistent bottleneck. If Clear Current's platform can handle this format variation more reliably than ENGIE's current OCR + manual keying workflow, that is a genuine competitive capability.
3.2 Exception Monitoring: Threshold-Based, Not Pattern-Based
ENGIE Impact's anomaly detection system compares incoming bills against a historical range. If a bill falls outside a set tolerance, it goes into an exception queue for review by the client.
Adam acknowledged the limitation of this approach directly: they flag the anomaly, but cannot diagnose why it happened. They do not have visibility into operational events at the site level — whether a pool was refilled, a kitchen deep clean was scheduled, or a meter was malfunctioning.
Implication for Clear Current: This is identical to the 'baseline drift' problem Roger Goldstein described at Panda. ENGIE's threshold-based system catches spikes but misses gradual creep. A pattern deviation detection model — not just a spike threshold — is the technical differentiation Clear Current should lead with in any QSR or managed services channel conversation.
3.3 Competitive Positioning: The Data Moat
When shown a competitor's platform (Arcadia), Adam's response was measured but strategically revealing. He acknowledged that the dashboards and sustainability analytics looked similar to ENGIE's own portals, but zeroed in on the structural difference: data access.
He went further to articulate ENGIE's moat directly: 'The hard part, the part that's not sexy or isn't fun, is in order to get that dashboard, you have to collect the data.' The AI-powered analytics that competitors showcase are layered on top of data that incumbents like ENGIE already possess.
Implication for Clear Current: This confirms what Marc Spieler identified as Track 3 — licensing into existing platforms. ENGIE Impact is the exact type of incumbent operator that a licensing or partnership conversation should target. Their client data + Clear Current's AI intelligence layer = a compelling combined offering that neither can build alone at the same quality.
3.4 Market Consolidation Dynamics
Adam noted that at enterprise scale — large organizations with a geographic footprint across the U.S. — there are very few credible outsourced bill processing providers. The mom-and-pop energy procurement shops and smaller advisory firms exist, but operating at the volume and accuracy required for a company like Panda is a high-barrier business. This market dynamic means a handful of large players (ENGIE Impact, NG Insight's parent, a few others) control the data for much of the large multi-site commercial market.
Implication for Clear Current: For Clear Current, the path into the large enterprise QSR and retail market may run through partnerships with these data holders rather than direct-to-customer sales. Whoever processes the bills controls the data. Getting embedded in that workflow — even as a white-labeled intelligence layer — could be more valuable than displacing the incumbent.
3.5 AI and the Sustainability Tension
Adam raised a point that has not come up in other interviews: as a sustainability company, ENGIE Impact is internally conflicted about its AI adoption because of the energy footprint of AI infrastructure itself. He noted that there are thoughtful internal conversations about when and how AI should be used given the company's stated mission.
Implication for Clear Current: This is an interesting flag for Clear Current's sales conversations in the sustainability-conscious segment. Framing Clear Current's AI platform as energy-efficient or as reducing wasted energy spend rather than adding to grid load could be a useful differentiator when engaging ESG-focused prospects.
4. Energy Market Intelligence from Adam
4.1 Long-Term Energy Price Trajectory
Adam provided an unprompted and confident macro view of U.S. energy prices: they will rise structurally over the next five to ten years regardless of short-term commodity fluctuations. The primary driver is a supply-demand imbalance that cannot be resolved quickly.
He specifically called out the upper Midwest and Northeast as regions seeing significant price increases already, and Texas as a grid reliability concern due to aging infrastructure and increasing severe weather events.
Implication for Clear Current: This macro framing strengthens the urgency behind Clear Current's pitch. Energy costs are going up regardless of what customers do on the efficiency side. The question is whether they can at least stop paying for energy they didn't consume. That framing — 'your costs are rising, but let's make sure every dollar you spend is actually justified' — plays directly into the anxiety these operators are experiencing.
4.2 The Waste and Recycling Parallel
Adam described how ENGIE Impact helps clients like Panda shop their waste contracts in deregulated markets — getting national haulers like Republic Services or Waste Management to bid on a portfolio. He also described bin right-sizing services: finding locations where waste pickups are too frequent relative to actual fill levels and adjusting schedules to reduce cost. The savings on a per-location basis seem small, but at Panda's scale of 2,600+ locations, they add up quickly.
Implication for Clear Current: This waste management use case is structurally identical to utility bill optimization: high volume, location-level variation, and chronic overpayment from default vendor relationships that nobody has time to audit. It validates the broader invoice auditing market that Marc Spieler described. The playbook Clear Current is running on energy bills could eventually extend to waste and recycling bills at the same multi-site operators.
5. Strategic Recommendations for Dan & John
Prepared by Clayton Hester  |  CDL MBA Consulting Team
Team: Clayton Hester, Adam Case, Jason Wisnieski, Victor Pan
Clear Current Technologies  |  Dan Schreiber (CPO) & John Reuter (CEO)
Why This Interview Matters
Adam Zavadsky is a Senior Account Manager at ENGIE Impact (formerly ENGIE Insight / Ecova), one of the largest outsourced utility bill processing and sustainability consulting firms in the United States. He manages a portfolio of enterprise clients that includes Panda Restaurant Group — the referral that connected him to this interview. With roughly 18 years at ENGIE Impact after a prior career in investment banking, Adam provides a uniquely financial and operational perspective on the managed energy services industry. His interview is the most direct source of intelligence on ENGIE Impact's business model, AI strategy, competitive positioning, and the structural advantages that incumbent bill processors hold over newer AI-native entrants.
Field
Detail
Name
Adam Zavadsky
Title
Senior Account Manager
Company
ENGIE Impact (formerly ENGIE Insight / Ecova)
Location
Saint Paul, MN
Education
BS, University of Minnesota Carlson School of Management
Prior Experience
Investment banking (Piper Jaffray, ~10 years)
Tenure at ENGIE
~18 years
Client Referral
Roger Goldstein, Executive Director Facilities & Energy, Panda Restaurant Group
Interview Method
Live call with Clayton Hester  |  April 14, 2026
"You can't manage what you can't measure. We can't help you if we don't pay your bills because otherwise we'd have no idea what we're doing."
"We're using AI to scrape data off of bills, to get invoices into our system more efficiently, without errors, less labor. We're not using it as much on the client deliverable side because those require a subject matter expert to collaborate with you on what we're going to do."
"We're looking at past history to see if it's within a certain tolerance or range. If it's out of range, we'll alert Panda to like, hey, normally your water is X and this bill we just got in is Y — is something wrong?"
"Arcadia — they don't pay bills, right? So how would they provide this service? They would need to either partner with someone who does pay bills, or they would need a centralized repository."
"We can't build enough supply to keep up with demand, whether that's AI data centers or just heating and cooling loads. It takes minimum five years to get a new natural gas electric power plant online."
Recommendation 1: Pursue a Partnership or Licensing Conversation with ENGIE Impact
ENGIE Impact has the client data; Clear Current has the AI intelligence layer. Adam's framing of the competitive landscape makes clear that new entrants need a data strategy, not just a product strategy. A conversation about embedding Clear Current's anomaly detection and tariff analysis capabilities within ENGIE Impact's existing client portal could create a channel partnership that bypasses the cold-start problem for Clear Current in the large QSR and retail segment.
Recommendation 2: Frame the Competitive Pitch Around Pattern Detection, Not Just Spike Alerts
ENGIE Impact's current system is threshold-based — it catches spikes but misses baseline drift. This is exactly the gap Roger Goldstein described when Panda paid a neighbor's electricity bill for an entire year. Clear Current's pitch to managed services clients and their enterprise customers should lead with 'we find what your current system can't' — the slow-burn errors that look normal until someone notices the baseline has quietly shifted upward.
Recommendation 3: Address the Municipality / Paper Bill Challenge as a Differentiator
Adam identified small-municipality water and sewer bills as the persistent data intake bottleneck — inconsistent formats, paper delivery, manual entry. If Clear Current can demonstrate that its AI can reliably extract data from varied paper bill formats without a manual keying step, that is a direct operational cost savings pitch for any outsourced bill processor considering Clear Current as a technology partner.
Recommendation 4: Use the Rising Energy Cost Macro in Sales Messaging
Adam's candid macro view — structural energy price increases locked in for the next five to ten years regardless of supply-side developments — is a powerful opening framing for Clear Current's sales conversations. 'Your energy costs are going up regardless of what you do. The question is whether you can at least stop paying for energy you didn't consume' is a more urgent pitch than any feature comparison.

═══ SOURCE: Clear_Current_Interview_Summary_AdventHealth.docx | FOLDER: Interview Summary Documents ═══
Interview Summary
AdventHealth Corporate | Sustainability & Energy Management
John Culver, Corporate Sustainability  &  Scott Sukits, Director of Control Systems & Energy Management
Conducted by Clayton Hester  |  April 15, 2026
1. Interviewee Profiles
John Culver — Corporate Sustainability
Scott Sukits — Director of Control Systems & Energy Management, Central Florida
Scott was hired specifically to bring energy management expertise to a division that previously lacked it. His prior 20 years as a consulting engineer give him an unusual dual perspective — he designed the systems that facilities teams now struggle to operate, and he now lives on the facilities side experiencing exactly those challenges firsthand.
2. AdventHealth — Energy Operations Overview
55 hospitals across 9 states; Central Florida Division alone covers 9 hospitals (soon 10) plus property management buildings
1,000–2,000 utility bills processed per day system-wide across electricity, gas, water, and telecom
73 different electric utility providers across 9 states — each with different bill formats, rate structures, and terminology
Third-party bill processor: ENGIE Impact (NG) — used primarily for bill payment, not analytics
BAS (Building Automation System): primarily Trane in Central Florida, plus some Siemens and Johnson Controls; JCI specialist on-site in Orlando
AI adoption at building operations level: machine learning pilots underway; full AI (BrainBox AI via Trane partnership) stuck in IT/cybersecurity approval for 2.5+ years
Natural gas hedging handled by a separate company on a rolling basis — currently under strategic review
Sustainability targets: 50% Scope 1 and 2 emissions reduction by 2030; net-zero by 2050; on track for 100% renewable electricity by 2026 via VPPAs and on-site solar
3. Key Interview Findings
3.1 The #1 Automation Wish: Bills Matching Addresses
When asked what one task they would automate immediately, John Culver's answer was immediate and surprisingly foundational — not an analytics feature, not a dashboard, but basic data accuracy.
He described a specific recurring problem: consumption occurs at one address but the bill is paid somewhere else. ENGIE Impact sometimes catches this split, sometimes doesn't — it depends on what the utility company sends. Mergers, acquisitions, new builds, and divestments constantly change the portfolio, and keeping billing addresses in sync with physical locations is a continuous, imperfect process.
Implication for Clear Current: This is the 'ghost meter' problem from the supply side. It is not just about catching inactive meters — it is about ensuring every dollar of energy consumption is correctly attributed to the right physical location. For a 55-hospital system in active M&A mode, this is not a one-time cleanup project; it is an ongoing, structural data integrity challenge. Clear Current's platform should explicitly position meter-to-address reconciliation as a core feature, not an edge case.
3.2 Scott's Automation Wish: Meter Data Automatically on a Dashboard
Scott's team currently has someone manually entering utility data into a portfolio management system. The ideal — automatic ingestion, automatic visualization, automatic anomaly flagging — is not yet real for them. The gap between what they have and what they want is not sophisticated AI; it is reliable, automated data pipelines.
Implication for Clear Current: Both John's and Scott's automation wishes point to the same root problem: data ingestion and attribution are still partially manual. The market is not asking for another analytics layer on top of bad data. It is asking for a platform that solves the data quality problem first and then delivers analytics on clean data. Clear Current's pitch in healthcare should lead with data integrity, not AI sophistication.
3.3 ENGIE Impact Gap: Analytical Depth vs. Bill Payment
AdventHealth has been using ENGIE Impact (NG) for utility bill payment for years. John was direct about what they get versus what they wish they had.
But when asked what he wishes ENGIE would do differently, three specific gaps emerged:
Tax exemption verification: As a non-profit, AdventHealth should not pay taxes on utility bills. Ensuring this is consistently applied across 73 utility providers and 55 hospitals is a gap that costs real money.
Portfolio change management: New sites from acquisitions and divestitures need to be added or removed from analytics quickly. Currently, this is slow and manual.
Intelligent bill interpretation: ENGIE aggregates bills and pays them, but does not explain the rate structures. With terminology ranging from on-peak, off-peak, discounted off-peak, super off-peak, and extreme on-peak, the bills are incomprehensible without expert interpretation — and ENGIE does not provide that layer.
Implication for Clear Current: This is the clearest direct statement of Clear Current's value proposition from any interview in the entire research program. John Culver described, unprompted and in his own words, exactly what Clear Current does: interpret what's on the bills and tell you why you should care and what you can do to change it. This quote should be in the final presentation.
3.4 AdventHealth Wants to Be the Expert, Not Just the Client
A key strategic nuance emerged: AdventHealth is not content to outsource energy intelligence to ENGIE. They want to develop internal expertise so they can respond faster and measure their own outcomes.
The monthly billing cycle is too slow for real-time operational decisions. John wants hourly or daily data — not the once-a-month snapshot ENGIE provides from utility bills.
Implication for Clear Current: This validates two product directions simultaneously. First, the near-real-time monitoring layer (AMI/interval data ingestion) is not just a nice-to-have — it is what sophisticated buyers like AdventHealth are explicitly asking for. Second, a platform that gives the internal team the tools to be their own experts is more appealing than one that positions itself as a replacement for their judgment. Clear Current should position as an intelligence platform that empowers the internal team, not another vendor they depend on.
3.5 Cybersecurity Is the Single Biggest AI Adoption Barrier
Scott's team has been trying to implement BrainBox AI (Trane's AI building optimization platform) at a medical office building for 2.5 years. The blocker is not budget, not technology, not interest — it is IT and cybersecurity approval.
The underlying concern is HIPAA: any system that creates a tunnel into the building controls network could theoretically be used to access patient data. The Johnson Controls hack accelerated these concerns dramatically.
Implication for Clear Current: This finding directly validates Phil Combs' warning from the Trane interview: IT is the gate, not the energy manager. For a billing intelligence platform like Clear Current, the cybersecurity barrier is lower than for a BAS-integrated AI system (Clear Current reads bills, not building controls networks), but SOC 2 compliance, data handling documentation, and a clear answer to 'who can see our data' are still required before any enterprise healthcare conversation. The pitch must explicitly separate Clear Current from the BrainBox/BAS category — 'we read your utility bills, not your building systems' is a meaningful differentiator.
3.6 Garbage In, Garbage Out — AI Requires Foundation First
Both John and Scott were skeptical of AI as a solution layered on top of existing broken data infrastructure. John articulated this most clearly:
Scott gave a vivid example: a sensor reporting a value because a physical wrench is holding a damper open. The AI reads the sensor data and says everything is fine. The AI cannot know the wrench is there.
Implication for Clear Current: This is not skepticism about AI per se — it is skepticism about AI as a shortcut. Both interviewees believe in AI's potential for trend spotting, anomaly detection, and root cause acceleration. But they have seen vendors oversell the 'just layer it on top' approach and fail. Clear Current's pitch to AdventHealth should acknowledge this directly: 'We start with data quality. Before we give you AI analytics, we make sure the data we're analyzing is right.' That credibility signal matters more to this buyer than any feature comparison.
3.7 The Capital Budgeting Problem — Data Must Arrive Before the Cycle
John described a structural timing problem that maps directly to the energy management calendar: capital improvement requests for energy projects must be submitted before the hospital's budgeting cycle, which requires having the energy data and operational ROI case assembled well in advance.
Implication for Clear Current: This is the internal champion problem described by Phil Combs, but from inside the organization. The energy team at AdventHealth must build a financial and operational case — not just an engineering case — to compete for capital against clinical priorities. A platform that automatically generates that case (energy trend data + anomaly history + projected ROI from rate optimization) is what gets a $1M capital improvement request approved, not just identified.
3.8 Florida Climate — No Seasonal Cycle, Humidity Is the Real Variable
Scott provided important geographic context for energy management in Central Florida: because the climate is warm year-round, traditional seasonal patterns (heat in winter, cool in summer) do not apply. The primary operational challenge is maintaining temperature and humidity within clinical compliance ranges regardless of season.
Scott described an enthalpy-based control approach that continuously modulates discharge air temperature based on actual space conditions — effectively a manual implementation of what AI control loops are designed to do automatically.
Implication for Clear Current: For the Seasonal Energy Management Calendar deliverable, AdventHealth's Florida hospitals are an outlier from the standard heating/cooling cycle. Their calendar is driven by regulatory compliance cycles (ENERGY STAR reporting, Joint Commission), capital budgeting timelines, and continuous HVAC compliance management — not seasonal peaks. This is worth noting in the calendar as a Southern/warm-climate variant.
4. Additional Context: AdventHealth's Broader Energy Strategy
Context from public sources that informed the interview framing:
Signed the HHS Health Sector Climate Pledge; targeting 50% Scope 1 and 2 emissions reduction by 2030 and net-zero by 2050
VPPAs with Scout Clean Energy (Texas wind, 40% of system electrical needs) and NextEra (solar); on track for 100% renewable electricity by 2026
3MW corporate campus solar installation completed March 2025 — one of Florida's largest privately owned solar projects
Watershed platform used for automated GHG data collection across the portfolio
Two sustainability co-leads at C-suite: Marisa Farabaugh (Chief Supply Chain Officer) and Rob Roy (Chief Investment Officer)
Each of the 55 hospitals has been asked to develop its own environmental sustainability plan
5. Key Strategic Recommendations for Dan & John
Prepared by Clayton Hester  |  CDL MBA Consulting Team
Team: Clayton Hester, Adam Case, Jason Wisnieski, Victor Pan
Clear Current Technologies  |  Dan Schreiber (CPO) & John Reuter (CEO)
Why This Interview Matters
This interview was originally scheduled with Allegra Wiesler, Executive Director of Environmental Sustainability at AdventHealth Corporate, who was unavailable. In her place, two colleagues with deep operational knowledge joined: John Culver from the corporate sustainability team and Scott Sukits, Director of Control Systems and Energy Management for the Central Florida Division (9 hospitals, growing to 10 by year end). Together they provide both the system-wide strategic view and the ground-level operational reality of energy management at one of the nation's largest faith-based health systems. This is one of the most operationally direct interviews in the research program for a critical finding: AdventHealth uses ENGIE Impact for bill processing, is dissatisfied with its analytical depth, and explicitly described what they wish it could do — a near-perfect articulation of what an AI-powered billing intelligence platform should offer.
Note on scheduled vs. actual participants: This interview was booked with Allegra Wiesler (Executive Director, Environmental Sustainability). She was unable to join. John Culver and Scott Sukits participated in her place. Their perspectives are operational and analytical — complementary to, but distinct from, the sustainability strategy lens Allegra would have brought. A follow-up interview with Allegra is recommended.

Scope
System-wide sustainability strategy; energy consumption tracking; portfolio-level analysis across all 55 hospitals in 9 states
Role in Interview
Provided the system-level strategic view: how AdventHealth thinks about energy data, the limitations of ENGIE Impact, and what they wish their tools could do
