Name
Scott Sukits
Title
Director of Control Systems and Energy Management, Central Florida Division
Company
AdventHealth Central Florida Division
Division
9 hospitals (growing to 10 by end of 2026) plus off-site EDs and property management buildings
Energy Team
3 energy managers (up from 2 at hire)
Additional Role
Also serves as Facility Director for AdventHealth East Orlando — split role
Background
Consulting Engineer for 20 years before joining AdventHealth; now on the facility operations side
Role in Interview
Provided the building operations and controls perspective: BAS optimization, AI adoption barriers, cybersecurity constraints, and what automation would save the most time
"It would be that the bills and the therms, deca therms, CCF, KWH, whatever, match the addresses of our sites every time. Which sounds basic, but again, with an organization this size and that grows, matching — hey, that electron or that piece of gas went into that site on this date, and I'm 100% positive of that fact — is foundational. You can't move forward if you don't know that information."
"The data input from the meters, if that just automatically populated to a dashboard that you could see trend data, which would be amazing. John has a team that enters information from the utility providers into portfolio management. That gives a pretty good outlook, but it would be nice to see all that come in, go on a chart, and then you could quickly see if you had a dashboard up if something's wrong."
"We use them for paying those thousand bills every day, and they do a great job of that. We didn't sign on with NG ages ago because of energy management needs. It was bill pay needs. And so they do what we pay them for very well."
"NG just aggregates those and pays the bottom line dollar. But it being a little bit smarter and knowing how to interpret what's on the bills and not just interpret, but tell us why we should care and what we can do to change it, would be great."
"AdventHealth is interested in being experts itself, so that we can triage it our own way for our own needs and then more rapidly measure the benefit after we do some sort of change on site. Like we want to be able to adjust the set point and then tomorrow check and see, okay, did that do what we thought it did? Versus waiting for a monthly bill."
"I've been trying to implement it at a medical office building for 2 and a half years now. And I've been working with our IT folks for that long to try to get it pushed through. And it's just question after questionnaire after questionnaire before they'll even consider approving it."
"Johnson Controls was hacked... and because that name was hacked, everything changed overnight. We went from being able to see our control dashboards to stuff just disappearing on our own dashboards in-house."
"Suppliers will make it sound like you can just layer it on top of your existing systems and it's fine. What we are finding is that is not the case. AI integration needs to be closer to the foundation because it changes so many things."
"AI is good, but it's only as good as what it's told."
"Having that information at hand and in time is crucial. In every company, there's a competition for capital. Facilities is less fun and less in your face... it's one of those, if it's working well, you don't notice until it fails. And so we have the job of trying to not be the boys who cried wolf, but say: here's what we suggest we do, and here's the benefits to doing so beyond just avoiding a catastrophe."
"In Florida, the reason we don't care about seasons all that much, we don't worry about heat too often. Our primary goal is to make sure we maintain temperature and humidity within critical spaces."
Recommendation 1: Use John Culver's Quote in Every Healthcare Pitch
"It being a little bit smarter and knowing how to interpret what's on the bills and not just interpret, but tell us why we should care and what we can do to change it, would be great." This is the most direct, unprompted articulation of Clear Current's value proposition from any interview in this research program. It came from an actual buyer at a 55-hospital system that currently uses ENGIE Impact and is dissatisfied. It should anchor the product module recommendations and the final presentation.
Recommendation 2: Lead with Data Integrity, Not AI Sophistication
Both John and Scott were skeptical of 'AI layered on top' as a solution. They have seen vendors overpromise this. Clear Current's pitch to AdventHealth should explicitly address the data quality problem first: 'Before we give you insights, we make sure your data is right.' Positioning around foundation-first credibility will resonate more than feature comparisons.
Recommendation 3: Make Non-Profit Tax Exemption a Named Feature
John identified tax exemption verification across 73 utility providers as a specific, ongoing pain point. AdventHealth is a non-profit and should not pay utility taxes — but consistently ensuring this across 73 different utility bills is a real operational gap. A billing audit that flags improper tax charges on non-profit accounts is a concrete, dollar-quantifiable feature with strong healthcare applicability.
Recommendation 4: Explicitly Separate Clear Current from BAS/Controls AI
Scott spent 2.5 years trying to get BrainBox AI through IT approval. The HIPAA and cybersecurity concerns around building controls network access are severe. Clear Current reads utility bills, not building control systems — it creates no HIPAA-relevant data tunnel. This distinction must be explicit in any AdventHealth or healthcare sales conversation: 'We analyze your invoices, not your building systems.'
Recommendation 5: Pursue the Allegra Wiesler Follow-Up
This interview was booked with Allegra Wiesler (Executive Director, Environmental Sustainability) and she did not attend. John and Scott provided excellent operational intelligence, but Allegra's perspective — as the person who owns sustainability strategy and directly oversees the emissions data workflows tied to the VPPA and renewable energy programs — would add a critical dimension around how billing data feeds into Scope 2 reporting. A 20-minute follow-up call with Allegra specifically on data quality for GHG reporting would complete the picture.
Recommendation 6: Build an M&A Portfolio Change Module
John described a constant pain point: as AdventHealth grows through mergers and acquisitions, new sites must be onboarded into billing analytics and old sites deactivated. ENGIE is slow to update. A platform that can rapidly onboard new meters, map them to addresses, and flag any mismatches from day one of an acquisition would directly address AdventHealth's most operationally painful gap.
═══ END: Clear_Current_Interview_Summary_AdventHealth.docx ═══



═══ SOURCE: Clear_Current_Interview_Summary_Andi_Ault_UT_Austin.docx | FOLDER: Interview Summary Documents ═══
Interview Summary
The University of Texas at Austin | Utilities & Energy Management
Andi Ault, Senior Associate Director, Finance & Administrative Services
Conducted by Clayton Hester  |  April 1, 2026
1. Interviewee Profile
Andi's career path is finance, not energy engineering. She came to UT Austin from a controller role at US Foods and a regulatory examiner role in North Dakota. Her expertise is in building efficient business processes, implementing financial controls, and providing financial analytics and forecasts. She was clear throughout the interview that she could speak to the financial realm but that energy operations, metering, and technical analysis were handled by other teams.
2. UT Austin - Energy Operations at a Glance
UT Austin's energy infrastructure is substantially more complex and self-sufficient than any other institution interviewed in this research program.
~95% on-site electricity generation on main campus via a combined heat and power plant with approximately 60 MW capacity. Only about 5% is purchased. This is fundamentally different from Texas State, which purchases all electricity from the City of San Marcos.
North Campus purchases electricity from the City of Austin and runs chilling operations there separately.
District energy system producing electricity, steam, and chilled water on-site and distributing to campus buildings.
~$20 million annual natural gas spend subject to fuel hedging strategy to manage market volatility.
200+ employees across five divisions with dedicated engineering, strategic programs, and finance teams.
Major capital expansion underway a new utility plant is needed to support a large medical building coming to campus.
PEER Platinum certified district energy system - one of the most awarded university energy operations in the country.
3. Key Interview Findings
3.1 Finance Is Reactive - Hedging Is the One Proactive Lever
Andi drew a clear line between the reactive nature of her financial work and the one proactive tool she has: fuel hedging. Most of her job involves taking historical data, updating it for current conditions, and building pro formas for upcoming situations.
Implication for Clear Current: The finance user is not looking at energy data to optimize operations - she is looking at it to predict and control costs. If Clear Current can provide forward-looking cost intelligence (rate trend analysis, regulatory monitoring, fuel price forecasting), it gives the finance user a second proactive lever beyond hedging.
3.2 The Dual-Audience Problem - Confirmed from the Finance Side
Andi explicitly confirmed the data tension that Andee Chamberlain described at Texas State, but from the opposite chair. The engineers and analysts do variance analysis through a technical lens; Andi sees the same data as dollars and consumption numbers.
When asked whether a tool should be cross-collaborative - where an energy expert chats with the data and shares reports to the finance user - Andi's response was immediate and emphatic: "Oh, absolutely. Absolutely."
Implication for Clear Current: This is the strongest validation yet of Dan's dual-audience UX design. The finance user does not want to do the technical analysis herself. She wants the technical team to interact with the tool, and then receive intuitive, shareable reports that translate findings into financial terms.
3.3 Anomaly Flagging Is the #1 Finance-Side Use Case
When asked how she would use an AI energy management platform, Andi's first and most specific answer was anomaly flagging - having the system surface irregularities rather than requiring someone to go looking for them.
Implication for Clear Current: From the finance side, the value proposition is not 'chat with your energy bill.' It is 'we flag what's wrong and hand you a shareable report.' The autonomous agent and Push to User dashboard layers are what sell to this persona.
3.4 Rate Accuracy and Meter-to-Bill Validation
Andi described a specific pain point around translating purchased energy rates into their internal billing system. Fixed costs versus volumetric costs skew the average rate month to month based on actual consumption.
Implication for Clear Current: This is a different error type than what Don or Andee described. It is not a wrong multiplier or a misdirected bill - it is a structural rate anomaly created by how fixed and variable costs interact across different consumption levels.
3.5 Metering Granularity Limits Financial Analysis
Andi revealed a metering gap that constrains what the finance team can do. For Education & General (E&G) buildings, they only have building-level metering - no sub-metering within buildings.
Implication for Clear Current: This echoes Andee Chamberlain's finding at Texas State about district energy metering challenges, but adds the finance dimension: the cost-benefit question of whether more granular metering is worth funding.
3.6 Financial Systems Are Outdated - Integration Is the Dream
When asked what one task she would want automated, Andi pointed not to utility systems but to financial systems. Her wish was for budgets and forecasts to be built within or integrated with their financial system.
Implication for Clear Current: Clear Current should consider how its outputs connect to financial systems, not just energy systems. If the platform can export budget-ready data or integrate with university ERP/financial platforms, that addresses a pain point the finance user cares about more than the chat interface.
4. Annual Budgeting Cycle - UT Austin
Key pain point: City of Austin rate changes are often finalized after UT Austin has already submitted its budget. Regulatory intelligence would directly address this by giving the finance team advance visibility into rate changes before they're finalized.
5. Follow-Up Contacts
Andi offered to connect the team with the operations and engineering side of UEM:
Ryan Thompson Executive Director, Utilities & Energy Management. Already on the outreach list; has not responded to emails.
Troy Nevels leader of a group Andi described as 'the most helpful' for energy management and operations questions. Andi will make the introduction.
M. Kai and Roberto other contacts on the original email thread who Andi confirmed are 'definitely the right people' for energy operations questions.
6. Strategic Recommendations for Dan & John
Prepared by Clayton Hester  |  CDL MBA Consulting Team
Team: Clayton Hester, Adam Case, Jason Wisnieski, Victor Pan
Clear Current Technologies  |  Dan Schreiber (CPO) & John Reuter (CEO)
Why This Interview Matters
Andi Ault is the first finance-side interviewee in this research program. Every previous interview (Don Johnson, Andee Chamberlain, Phil Combs) came from the energy operations or solutions provider perspective. Andi provides the missing half of the dual-audience problem identified across all prior interviews: the person who sees energy data as dollars, budgets, and forecasts rather than demand curves and consumption profiles. UT Austin operates one of the most advanced district energy systems in the country, generating approximately 95% of main campus electricity on-site with 60 MW capacity. Her perspective on how financial systems, budgeting cycles, and cost forecasting interact with energy data is uniquely valuable for Clear Current's product design.

Andi Ault

Senior Associate Director, Finance & Administrative Services

Utilities & Energy Management (UEM), UT Austin Campus Operations
Contact
512-738-1211

Controller at US Foods (large division) | Financial Institutions Examiner, State of North Dakota

Role in Interview
Provided finance-side perspective on budgeting, forecasting, fuel hedging, cost analysis, and financial system limitations
"For me, the finance is really reactive. We can take other measures that are proactive like for instance hedging our fuel. We buy $20 million in natural gas every year plus or minus depending on pricing at the time."
"Our engineers and analysts within our organization, they do go through all the variance analysis. I don't look at that. That's their job because they would see it through a different lens than I would - they have the direct line to, mechanically, what's happening within our own plant or distribution systems. For me it's just dollars and consumption numbers."
"Definitely flagging anomalies would be the first process so that that is streamlined and featured and flagged rather than looking for it. That would be time saving."

"We don't have great granularity. It's just building-level metering because nobody's looking at those statements except for us - we're billing ourselves. Could we have better granularity? If we funded more metering we could, but what is the cost benefit?"
"Our financial systems are pretty outdated. Just getting that back-end data isn't always the easiest. The ability to build budgets and forecasts within our financial system - or have it be integrated with our financial system - would be very helpful."

October
Budget planning begins for the next fiscal year. Finance team assembles cost components: fuel forecasts, staffing levels, salary/wages, new building projections, distribution project costs.
Oct - Feb
Ongoing budget development. Five-year forecast built alongside the annual budget. Fuel hedging strategy reviewed and executed. Pro formas compiled for capital projects.
March
Budgets delivered. However, City of Austin rate changes are often not known until after budget submission, creating a forecasting gap.
Sep 1
New fiscal year begins. Approved budgets take effect.
Ongoing
Monthly: pulling reports, running trends, comparing similar facilities for forecasting. Continuous: validation of purchased utility bills. Multi-year: fuel hedging strategy, capital planning.
Recommendation 1: Design Finance-Specific Outputs, Not Just Chat
Andi confirmed that the finance user does not want to chat with energy data - she wants anomalies flagged and shareable reports generated automatically. Clear Current's product for the finance persona should emphasize the Push to User dashboard and automated report generation, not the chat interface. The chat is for the energy expert; the finance user wants outputs.
Recommendation 2: Build Rate Anomaly Detection Across Comparable Buildings
Andi described a specific pain point: fixed vs. volumetric cost blending skews rates month to month, making it hard to tell if a building is being charged correctly without comparing it to an equivalent facility. If Clear Current can automatically flag when a building's blended rate deviates from comparable buildings, that is a finance-specific insight that directly addresses her workflow.
Recommendation 3: Consider Financial System Integration as a Product Roadmap Item
Andi's #1 automation wish was not about energy tools - it was about financial systems. Her back-end financial infrastructure is outdated, and she wants budgets and forecasts integrated with or exportable to her financial system. Clear Current should consider how its outputs connect to ERP and financial platforms, not just energy platforms.
Recommendation 4: Use Regulatory Intelligence to Close the Budget Timing Gap
UT Austin submits its budget before knowing the City of Austin's final rates. If Clear Current can surface upcoming rate changes from PUC filings and utility board decisions before budget deadlines, it gives the finance user a forecasting advantage they currently lack.
Recommendation 5: Pursue the Troy Nevels Introduction
Andi offered to connect the team with Troy Nevels, the operations-side leader she described as most helpful. A follow-up interview with his team would complete the UT Austin picture: Andi gave the finance perspective, and Troy's team can give the energy operations perspective at the same institution.

═══ SOURCE: Clear_Current_Interview_Summary_Ann_Walston_Bon_Secours (1).docx | FOLDER: Interview Summary Documents ═══
Interview Summary
Ann Walston | Bon Secours Mercy Health
Vice President, Acute Care Facilities Management
Conducted by Clayton Hester  |  April 14, 2026
1. Interviewee Profile
Ann's background is in facilities and real estate operations at enterprise scale, not energy engineering. She came to BSMH from CBRE and brings a business operator's perspective to energy management — focused on budgeting, procurement strategy, vendor relationships, and organizational accountability. She chairs a Utilities Procurement Steering Committee (UPSC) that sets hedging strategy for both natural gas and electricity. She was working on natural gas contract execution the same day as this interview.
2. BSMH — Energy Operations at a Glance
36 fully owned U.S. hospitals; all electricity and natural gas purchased from the grid (no on-site generation)
Utilities Procurement Steering Committee (UPSC) sets procurement strategy quarterly; includes facilities and finance representatives, plus an external market consultant
Hedging strategy: BSMH layers hedges over time (e.g., 25% locked at one price point, then additional tranches as markets move) to achieve full-year price predictability
One certified energy manager on staff — responsible for building-level efficiency roadmaps and annual consumption reduction targets
All utility bills routed to third-party processor Get Choice, which receives, analyzes, and pays invoices
Energy Star program used as the primary measurement framework: each hospital has a 1-year, 5-year, and 10-year Energy Star target; scores update monthly as bills come in
Hospitals in Ireland are governed by statute on energy management and are described as more advanced than U.S. locations
3. Key Interview Findings
3.1 Finance Has Limited Levers — Hedging Is the One Proactive Tool
Ann drew a clear parallel to what Andi Ault described at UT Austin: the finance-side energy role is largely reactive, with hedging strategy as the primary means of controlling costs proactively. The day of this interview, BSMH was executing on natural gas supplier contracts that would enable a new round of hedges — with the market at favorable levels, she was deciding whether to lock in additional tranches.
Implication for Clear Current: This confirms the finance-user pattern now seen across three healthcare systems and two universities: the person managing energy costs is primarily concerned with cost predictability, not operational efficiency. Clear Current's pitch to this persona should center on the regulatory intelligence and rate trend features that give the finance team forward visibility — not on the chat interface or anomaly detection, which are the energy manager's tools.
3.2 Billing Errors Are Real — And Recovery Is Rare
Ann provided the most detailed and candid account of a major billing error recovery of any interviewee in this research program. Over her 10-12 years in energy procurement across multiple health systems, she has encountered two significant meter failures that resulted in millions of dollars of overbilling.
She described the first incident as approximately $5-6 million in overbilling, with partial recovery. The second was a $1 million recovery, achieved only because BSMH had board-level relationships with the utility company that could be escalated to resolve the disputed final portion.
Her description of the mechanics was revealing: the utility had received approximately $900,000 of the $1 million paid, but the remaining amount had 'gone into line losses' — a settlement mechanism where the exact dollars are difficult to trace. Recovery required board-to-board negotiation, not a standard dispute process.
Implication for Clear Current: This is the most concrete validation yet of the billing error recovery challenge that Marc Spieler flagged: it is not enough to identify the error. The utility must acknowledge it and issue a credit, and that process is political, not algorithmic. Clear Current's case studies need to document the full recovery cycle — error found, utility engaged, credit issued, dollars returned — not just the error identification. The Ann Walston example also shows that recovery is harder than finding the error. Healthcare organizations in particular may benefit from Clear Current's involvement in the utility engagement process, not just the analysis.
3.3 Capacity Charges Are the Healthcare-Specific Pain Point
Ann unprompted raised capacity charges as the most urgent and least controllable billing issue for hospitals in the PJM region (the mid-Atlantic and Midwest grid operator). She described a tenfold increase in capacity charges over the prior two years — driven by data center construction in Ohio and neighboring states creating massive new load on the grid.
The 15-minute interval demand peak — the same mechanism Don Johnson described in West Texas — sets capacity charge levels for healthcare institutions in PJM territory. Ann specifically noted that managing demand peaks in a hospital is structurally harder than in a commercial building: 'I'd really like you to do it after five... that's really a tough thing to manage versus when I worked outside of healthcare, we literally could push and pull our operations to manage the capacity charge.'
Implication for Clear Current: This is a healthcare-specific capacity management insight that no other interviewee has provided. The 15-minute interval demand peak is a billing intelligence opportunity — but Clear Current should not position it as a 'reduce your peak' solution in healthcare, because hospitals cannot easily shed load. The correct positioning is: 'we identify when you're at risk of a capacity ratchet and surface the root cause, even if you can't change the behavior.' The value is documentation and regulatory awareness, not demand reduction.
3.4 The Meter Mapping Problem — A Year's Worth of Work
Ann described a foundational data infrastructure challenge that has not appeared in any other interview: mapping meters to buildings. BSMH hospitals have grown through additions and expansions, often with multiple meters feeding a single building or a single meter partially serving multiple buildings. Establishing which meter feeds which wing of which hospital — a 'meter study' — took over a year for her team to complete.
Implication for Clear Current: Before a healthcare system can use any energy intelligence platform effectively, they need to know which meter maps to which building and care area. Clear Current should consider whether it can assist with or accelerate meter-to-building mapping as part of onboarding, or whether this prerequisite work is a barrier that limits the short-term addressable market in healthcare. Systems with completed meter studies (like BSMH, after a year's effort) are ready buyers; systems that haven't done this work are not yet ready.
3.5 The Single Energy Manager Constraint
BSMH has one certified energy manager responsible for 36 hospitals. He physically travels to each hospital, observes building systems, and builds facility-specific efficiency roadmaps. This is exactly the 'Fake Energy Manager' problem, but from the opposite direction — BSMH has a highly competent person in the role, but the staffing ratio makes systematic attention to any single location impossible.
When asked how she would prioritize an energy AI platform, Ann's answer aligned perfectly with the finance-user pattern identified earlier: she wants to know where to invest dollars for maximum efficiency impact across a 36-hospital portfolio.
Implication for Clear Current: This is the ROI prioritization use case: not 'what's wrong' but 'where should I spend money to fix things.' A platform that can rank a hospital portfolio by efficiency opportunity — accounting for Energy Star score, equipment age, capacity charge exposure, and historical billing anomalies — would give BSMH's single energy manager a data-driven work queue. That prioritization layer is the product BSMH needs, and currently cannot build themselves at scale.
4. Annual Energy Management Calendar — BSMH
Key observation: Hospital load profiles are highly predictable — BSMH knows exactly when it will be running chillers, when it will need boilers, and what its seasonal consumption baseline should be. Deviations from this predictable profile are meaningful signals of equipment failure, meter errors, or billing irregularities.
5. Strategic Recommendations for Dan & John
Prepared by Clayton Hester  |  CDL MBA Consulting Team
Team: Clayton Hester, Adam Case, Jason Wisnieski, Victor Pan
Clear Current Technologies  |  Dan Schreiber (CPO) & John Reuter (CEO)
Why This Interview Matters
Ann Walston is VP of Acute Care Facilities Management at Bon Secours Mercy Health, one of the nation's 20 largest health systems, overseeing 36 U.S.-owned hospitals. She is a recognized leader in healthcare sustainability, named a 2025 ASHE Sustainability Champion and widely cited in industry publications for BSMH's Energy Star achievements. Ann is a career facilities executive — she worked previously in a VP role at CBRE — and now manages the full spectrum of hospital facilities strategy including procurement, consumption management, energy budgeting, and third-party vendor relationships. Her interview is the most senior healthcare-specific voice in this research program, and the most specific source on how utility billing errors actually surface and get resolved at scale.
Field
Detail
Name
Ann Walston
Title
Vice President, Acute Care Facilities Management
Company
Bon Secours Mercy Health (BSMH)
Location
Columbus, Ohio (HQ: Cincinnati, Ohio)
Prior Experience
Senior facilities and real estate roles at CBRE; VP Facilities at other healthcare organizations
Tenure at BSMH
~5 years
Hospitals
36 fully owned U.S.-based acute care hospitals; 48 total including partnership and international (Ireland)
Recognition
2025 ASHE Sustainability Champion; Energy Star Program advocate
Third-Party Processor
Get Choice (bill processing) — receives, enters, and analyzes all utility invoices
Interview Method
Live call with Clayton Hester  |  April 14, 2026
"We value predictability and pricing stability. Sometimes we pay a little bit of a premium for that, and we're okay with that."
"We recuperated over $1,000,000 from the utility company. And that is incredibly unheard of. I mean, that is incredibly rare."
"Our capacity charges went up tenfold in the last two years and they're anticipating it to go up again. Short answer is data centers."
"That was over a year's worth of work, just to identify who's got what... just to identify what's influencing what. Then you get into, okay, can I pay my bills on time?"
"In all reality, I would be looking for where to invest the dollars that I have to work on energy consumption reduction. Where's the most impact?"
Period
Activity
Spring (Mar-May)
Chillers come online (sometimes March, always by April). Shoulder season: free cooling utilized where possible. Boiler maintenance occurs over summer months.
Summer (Jun-Sep)
Peak electricity consumption; chillers running full capacity. Boiler repairs and replacements take place — this is the off-season for heating equipment.
Fall (Sep-Nov)
Chillers shut down; boilers come back online. Shoulder season optimization: maximize outside air, minimize both heating and cooling energy.
Winter (Dec-Feb)
Boilers running at peak. Cooling equipment maintenance and replacements occur — off-season for chillers.
Quarterly
UPSC (Utilities Procurement Steering Committee) meets to review market positions, hedging strategy, and procurement decisions for natural gas and electricity.
Ongoing
Monthly Energy Star score updates per hospital as bills arrive. Continuous: building-level efficiency roadmap execution by the single certified energy manager. Annual: prior-year ESG/sustainability reporting.
Recommendation 1: Lead Healthcare Pitches with Billing Error Documentation, Not Just Detection
Ann's most vivid story was not about detecting an error — it was about recovering dollars after one. She fought for $1M+ over months, with board-level intervention, and still lost some. Clear Current's healthcare pitch should go beyond 'we find errors' to 'we document, quantify, and help you pursue recovery with the utility.' That's the value the VP of Facilities needs to take to her leadership team.
Recommendation 2: Build a Capacity Charge Intelligence Module for PJM/Northeast Customers
Ann's tenfold capacity charge increase driven by data center construction is a healthcare-specific pain point tied to the 15-minute interval demand peak. In healthcare, demand management isn't about behavioral changes — it's about knowing when you're at risk, understanding why, and surfacing it to the right people before the bill arrives. A proactive capacity alert module would resonate immediately with hospital systems in PJM territory.
Recommendation 3: Develop an Onboarding Framework for Meter Mapping
Ann spent a year mapping meters to buildings before she could get meaningful insights from her system. Clear Current should build an onboarding playbook that either assists with or accelerates this process — or be explicit in sales conversations about the prerequisite. For a new health system customer, the question 'do you have a completed meter-to-building map?' may be the single best qualification filter for who is ready to buy vs. who needs 12 months of prep work first.
Recommendation 4: Position Around Portfolio ROI Prioritization, Not Just Anomaly Alerts
Ann's #1 question is: where do I invest my limited dollars and my one energy manager's time for maximum efficiency impact? A feature that ranks hospitals by opportunity — combining Energy Star score trajectory, billing anomaly frequency, capacity charge exposure, and equipment age signals — would directly answer her question in a way that no current tool does.
Recommendation 5: Reference BSMH as Proof That Healthcare Is Ready for This Category
Ann asked to see the research output from this program. She acknowledged direct experience with meter-level billing errors resulting in $1M+ recovery conversations. She is interested in better tools. With appropriate permissions and sanitization, BSMH's use of an AI-powered anomaly detection system (Get Choice) as validation that healthcare has already accepted this category — and the door is open for platforms that go further.

═══ SOURCE: Clear_Current_Interview_Summary_Intermountain_Health_Team (1).docx | FOLDER: Interview Summary Documents ═══
Interview Summary
Intermountain Health | Energy Management Team
Ross Snow, Matt Wilson & Bart Peacock
Conducted by Clayton Hester  |  April 14, 2026
1. Interviewee Profiles
Primary: Ross Snow — Director of Energy Management
Supporting: Matt Wilson — Manager, Remote Operations Center (ROC)
Supporting: Bart Peacock — Manager, Support Services
Intermountain Health is a large, nonprofit health system based in Salt Lake City with facilities spanning Utah, Colorado, and beyond. The energy management department reports into facility management and is responsible for the full energy lifecycle: production efficiency, capital equipment replacement, building system optimization, utility procurement, and sustainability reporting. The team of 14 — most in Utah, two on Colorado's front range — is one of the largest hospital energy programs in the country.
2. Intermountain Health — Energy Program Overview
14 energy management professionals — the largest internal healthcare energy team encountered in this research program
Remote Operations Center (ROC) monitors building automation systems (BAS) across all facilities 24/7
14 billion data points currently stored in SkySpark analytics platform, sourced from HVAC, lighting, BAS, fire, and elevator systems
Program saves more than twice what it costs to operate annually, including all salary, operating budget, and equipment costs
Work is divided into optimization (making current systems run better) and capital replacement (replacing aged equipment on the opposite seasonal schedule from when it operates)
A third party should have flagged utility bills that spiked from ~$40,000 to ~$140,000 — they didn't. An internal accountant caught it. This directly motivated AI capability discussion.
3. Key Interview Findings
3.1 The Two-Layer Model: Optimization vs. Capital Replacement
Ross provided the clearest articulation in this research program of how a sophisticated hospital energy team structures its calendar and work. He described two distinct layers:
The first layer is optimization work — making existing equipment run as efficiently as possible. This follows a seasonal calendar: winter is spent improving heating systems, summer is spent improving cooling systems, and shoulder seasons (spring and fall) are the most complex periods because systems are switching modes and managers must optimize for both heating and cooling simultaneously.
The second layer is capital replacement — replacing equipment at end-of-service-life. This runs on the opposite seasonal calendar: cooling equipment gets replaced in winter (when it's not running), heating equipment gets replaced in summer.
Implication for Clear Current: This two-layer structure is directly relevant to the Seasonal Energy Management Calendar deliverable. For a hospital energy team, the procurement and consumption calendar is not primarily driven by budget cycles — it is driven by equipment seasonality and the trade-off between running equipment and maintaining it.
3.2 The Bottleneck Is Not Data — It Is Boots on the Ground
The most emphatic and repeated insight from this call is that Intermountain's biggest constraint is not analytical capability or data access — it is the availability of skilled trade workers to physically fix the problems that data analysis identifies.
Matt showed a live example during the call: SkySpark had flagged a VAV damper failure in a patient room — the system was calling for 100% airflow but delivering less than 50 CFM. The diagnosis (no power to the damper actuator) required a physical technician to open the ceiling tile, inspect the unit, and trace the electrical issue. The AI could identify the symptom; only a person could find the cause.
Implication for Clear Current: This is not a criticism of AI platforms — it is a scope clarification that Clear Current should take seriously. In the hospital HVAC and building operations segment, the value proposition of an AI tool is helping the right technician get to the right problem faster, not replacing the technician. Clear Current's billing intelligence product is correctly scoped to the supply side (what you're being charged for) rather than the demand side (physical equipment operations). That distinction matters enormously for how the platform should be positioned in healthcare conversations.
3.3 Where AI Tooling Does Have Value in Hospital Energy Management
Despite the bottleneck critique, Ross was direct that AI has genuine and near-term value in specific areas:
Utility bill anomaly detection: Ross described a Colorado hospital where monthly electric bills of ~$40,000 jumped to ~$140,000. 'A third party should have flagged that — they didn't.' An accountant caught it manually. AI that reliably surfaces these anomalies without requiring manual review is directly valuable.
Pattern-level diagnosis across intertwined systems: Ross's most insightful point: when a facility team sees a symptom in one piece of equipment, they often spend days chasing it before discovering the root cause is in an entirely different intertwined system. AI that can look at all symptoms across all equipment in a facility simultaneously and point to the most likely root cause — not just the symptom — would save significant diagnostic time.
Work order pre-qualification: Matt described a specific use case: when a complaint comes in ('room 414 is hot'), an AI layer that can immediately pull the last 30 days of data for that room, assess whether it's a new or recurring issue, and run basic diagnostics (is airflow low? is the damper position consistent?) would give technicians a head start before they go on-site.
Implication for Clear Current: These use cases are adjacent to but distinct from Clear Current's core billing intelligence product. They are inside-the-meter (equipment operations) rather than outside-the-meter (utility billing). However, they establish a clear mental model for what hospital energy teams value from an AI system: pre-qualification, diagnosis support, and proactive pattern detection — not dashboards for their own sake.
3.4 The Blue Collar Shortage — A Strategic Research Flag
Ross raised an unprompted and emphatic point about a structural challenge that is broader than any single organization: the skilled trade worker shortage in facility management. He described Intermountain as willing to hire immediately at six-figure salaries for experienced building automation controls technicians, with no college degree required — and still being unable to fill the positions.
He connected this directly to the AI hype cycle: Intermountain receives pitches from AI companies constantly, but they do not need more analytics. They need people who can go fix the problems the analytics are already surfacing.
Implication for Clear Current: This is not directly relevant to Clear Current's billing product, but it is a significant market intelligence finding: the healthcare facility management industry is structurally understaffed on the physical execution side. This means energy problems that analytics can identify will remain unresolved for longer in hospitals than in commercial or industrial settings — making prevention and early detection through billing signals (catching issues before they escalate) even more valuable.
4. Annual Energy Management Calendar — Intermountain Health
5. Strategic Recommendations for Dan & John
Prepared by Clayton Hester  |  CDL MBA Consulting Team
Team: Clayton Hester, Adam Case, Jason Wisnieski, Victor Pan
Clear Current Technologies  |  Dan Schreiber (CPO) & John Reuter (CEO)
Why This Interview Matters
Intermountain Health's energy management team is the most sophisticated and deeply staffed energy program encountered in this research program. With 14 full-time energy professionals — compared to a single certified energy manager at organizations like BSMH — Intermountain has built what Ross Snow describes as a program that saves twice what it costs to operate every year. This interview is uniquely valuable not for a profile of an energy tool buyer, but for a ground-level understanding of what best-in-class hospital energy management actually looks like. That understanding reveals precisely where even a sophisticated internal team has gaps — and where AI tooling would add value rather than substitute for it.

"Where the rubber really hits the road is not identifying the problems, it's fixing them. And that's where companies, they will come tell you all the reasons your building is inefficient and why you could save $100,000 a year — but they won't solve them."
"We have a glutton of AI developers and a vacuum of problem solvers right now."
"If the AI were to able to plow through the data and say, okay, this is a new problem because the room has been within temperature 99% of the time for the last month... I think that would be interesting to me because it would free up their time."
"I cannot overstate how short that staffing is across our country right now. We would literally hire somebody tomorrow if they could fill those skill sets."
Period
Activity
Winter
Heating optimization; cooling equipment maintenance and capital replacement (off-season for chillers and air conditioning)
Spring/Fall (Shoulder)
Most complex periods: systems switching between heating and cooling modes. Optimization for outside air utilization (economizer mode). Matt: 'The shoulder seasons are the difficult ones — you may be heating in the morning, cooling in the afternoon.'
Summer
Cooling optimization; heating equipment maintenance and capital replacement (off-season for boilers and heat pumps)
Ongoing — ROC
24/7 monitoring of 14 billion data points via SkySpark. Automated analytics flag issues and route work orders to on-site facilities teams.
Ongoing — Capital
Capital improvement projects planned on opposite schedule from season of use. New equipment integrated with updated controls and sequences to optimize efficiency.
Ongoing — Procurement
Natural gas procurement strategy managed by facility management team; seeks favorable pricing windows.
Recommendation 1: Clearly Scope the Product as Supply-Side, Not Demand-Side
Intermountain's inside-the-meter capabilities (SkySpark, ROC, 14 billion data points) are dramatically more sophisticated than what any billing intelligence platform would add. The conversation that landed in this interview was not 'here's a tool that will help you run your buildings better' — it was 'here's a tool that helps you know if your bills are right.' That distinction should be explicit in all healthcare pitches. Clear Current operates outside the meter; inside-the-meter systems like SkySpark are not competitors — they are complementary.
Recommendation 2: Lead with the Utility Anomaly Case That Slipped Through
Ross volunteered the story of utility bills that jumped from $40K to $140K and were missed by the third-party processor. That story is exactly the pitch. 'Your monitoring platform is excellent for equipment operations. But your utility billing anomalies are checked by someone else — and they missed a $100,000 spike. We catch what they miss.' That framing positions Clear Current as additive to Intermountain's existing stack, not competitive with it.
Recommendation 3: Pursue Intermountain as a Healthcare Reference Customer
Ross, Matt, and Bart are genuinely curious about AI's role in energy management and were thoughtful and specific about where they see value. An organization with this level of sophistication and this many data points would be a high-credibility reference customer for the healthcare vertical — particularly because their validation comes with analytical credibility that a less sophisticated buyer cannot provide.

═══ SOURCE: Clear_Current_Interview_Summary_Jamare_Bates_JLL.docx | FOLDER: Interview Summary Documents ═══
Interview Summary
Jamare Bates  |  JLL
Vice President, Clean Energy & Infrastructure Advisory
Conducted by Clayton Hester & Victor Pan  |  April 2026
1. Interviewee Profile
Jamare’s career path is unusual in this research program. He spent the first half of his career in horizontal construction and federal infrastructure — roads, bridges, highways, military installations — before pivoting into clean energy advisory. He spent a decade at Black & Veatch building their federal EV infrastructure initiative from scratch, functioning as an entrepreneur within a large engineering firm. He joined JLL in July 2023 as part of a publicly announced expansion of JLL’s Clean Energy & Infrastructure Advisory team, which was responding to surging demand from corporate and public sector clients.
His current focus at JLL spans two primary areas: energy savings and efficiency advisory for commercial real estate clients, and EV fleet conversion strategy. He works primarily with large private sector clients — Fortune 200 and Fortune 500 companies — though public sector clients, state and local governments, and higher education institutions are also part of his client base.
2. Market Context & Macro Framing
Jamare opened the interview with one of the most expansive macro framings offered by any interviewee in this research program. His perspective reflects a long career watching energy markets shift from a cost afterthought to a primary strategic constraint.
2.1 Energy Is Becoming the #1 Site Selection Factor
Jamare described a fundamental shift in how corporations select real estate locations. Historically, the primary questions were about land cost and workforce availability. That calculus has changed:
The question now is: where is the cheapest energy? Workforce will follow.
Data centers and AI have driven exponential growth in energy demand, compared to what was historically a slow, steady annual increase.
Middle Eastern conflicts are accelerating energy cost pressures that JLL had been forecasting for two to three years. Clients who dismissed long-term energy risk are now flooding in looking for help.
2.2 The Antiquated Facilities Problem
Jamare described a structural energy efficiency gap across the U.S. commercial real estate portfolio:
Most U.S. commercial real estate has been managed under a retrofit-first strategy, meaning buildings are upgraded incrementally rather than replaced or overhauled.
Legacy buildings were built when energy was cheap, so efficiency was never a primary design constraint. A building that was 70% efficient was acceptable when energy costs were low.
Now that energy costs have escalated sharply, the inefficiency of those buildings has become a major financial liability — and many clients are locked into multi-year leases with no easy exit.
The office vacancy crisis compounds this: buildings that are 35% occupied are losing money, while the cost to retrofit them to Class A standard (to attract tenants back) is often prohibitive.
3. JLL’s Energy Advisory Practice
3.1 Team Origin and Mandate
JLL’s Clean Energy & Infrastructure Advisory team is relatively new — six to eight years old in its current form. It was stood up in the 2016–2018 timeframe in response to the early growth of the EV market and shifting client questions about clean energy infrastructure. The team now has 40+ energy and finance professionals.
JLL’s rationale for building in-house expertise rather than outsourcing it is straightforward: every piece of real estate requires energy, and clients were asking questions that required deep technical expertise. Rather than referring clients elsewhere, JLL chose to build the capability internally.
3.2 What the Team Actually Does
Energy savings advisory: identifying retrofit opportunities, demand reduction strategies, and efficiency upgrades
EV fleet conversion: advising corporate clients on transitioning vehicle fleets to electric, regardless of federal tax credit availability
Battery energy storage: advising on backup power and grid resilience solutions
Solar: advising building owners on rooftop and on-site solar deployment
P3 and project finance: advising on non-recourse financing, public-private partnerships, and municipal financings for infrastructure projects
Federal program management: JLL has won large multi-year contracts with federal agencies including HUD
3.3 JLL’s In-House AI Tooling
This is the most important section for Clear Current’s positioning. Jamare confirmed that JLL has an internal technology division — JLL Technologies — that builds AI and data tools for the firm. JLL already has or is building tools that analyze utility and billing data for clients. Key details:
JLL has property management software, facility management software, and building automation system integrations that generate enormous volumes of operational and energy data.
JLL has found “millions and millions of dollars” in client savings using a suite of AI and human-in-the-loop tools — not a single tool, but an integrated stack.
The ability to do this at scale exists because JLL already has the data. Managing client facilities and properties means they already hold the utility invoice history and consumption data.
Implication: At the JLL tier of the market, the conversation is not about whether to buy a point solution. It is about acquisition or enterprise licensing — and even then, the more likely outcome Jamare described is acquisition.
4. Clear Current Fit Assessment
4.1 Market Segmentation Framework (from Jamare)
Jamare spontaneously offered a three-tier market segmentation framework when asked about the AI billing tool:
When told Clear Current is targeting multi-location retailers (Home Depots, CVS-type companies), Jamare confirmed that profile aligns with the mid-market and upper-mid-market tier where a dedicated software tool creates the most value.
4.2 The Data Access Problem
Jamare identified data access as the single most critical barrier for an AI billing tool attempting to enter the enterprise segment:
Large clients are extremely reluctant to share utility invoice data with third parties. They do not want their energy spend visible in the market.
Fear of AI model training on proprietary data is a real and growing concern at the enterprise level. Clients will ask directly whether their data is being used to train models shared with others.
Security, anonymization, and data governance will be the first questions any serious procurement team asks. This is not a checkbox — it is a make-or-break qualification.
Implication for Clear Current: Data privacy and security architecture need to be front-facing — not buried in a terms of service. For enterprise and mid-market prospects, Jamare’s framing suggests this is a sales conversation, not just a legal one.
4.3 Energy Auditors as Both Target Users and Displaced Incumbents
One of the most actionable recommendations Jamare offered was to interview current energy auditors. His reasoning:
Energy auditors currently perform much of what the AI billing tool is trying to automate — by hand.
If the tool is positioned as a job replacement, auditors will be adversarial toward it and will resist adoption wherever they have procurement influence.
If the tool is positioned as a force multiplier — helping auditors do more with less manual effort — it converts a potential obstacle into an early adopter and internal champion.
This directly mirrors Don Johnson’s “fake energy manager” insight: the person who looks like an obstacle is often the person who needs the product most.
5. Key Quotes
6. Research Gaps & Recommended Follow-Up
Energy auditor interviews: Jamare specifically recommended this as the highest-value next step. Current auditors are both the functional incumbents and the most likely early adopters if the tool is positioned correctly.
JLL as a channel or distribution partner: Jamare’s framing of JLL’s capabilities and client base suggests a potential channel relationship worth exploring — particularly if Clear Current targets mid-market clients that JLL serves but does not build custom tools for.
Warm intro potential: Jamare offered to stay connected and suggested follow-up. Worth a direct ask for an introduction to energy auditors in his network or other mid-market energy managers.
Confidential | MBA Research Engagement — Texas A&M Mays Business School
CDL-Texas Energy Stream | Clear Current Technologies | Clayton Hester, Jason Wisnieski, Adam Case, Victor Pan
Why This Interview Matters
Jamare Bates is a Vice President on JLL’s Clean Energy & Infrastructure Advisory team, one of the most prominent commercial real estate advisory firms in the world with over 100,000 employees globally. His background spans 25 years of engineering, federal program management, and clean energy advisory work, giving him a rare cross-sector lens on how large institutions actually buy and deploy energy solutions. His team advises Fortune 500 companies, federal agencies, and higher education institutions on decarbonization, EV fleet transition, and energy efficiency, placing him in direct contact with the types of organizations Clear Current is targeting. This interview surfaces critical intelligence on data access barriers, enterprise AI tool adoption patterns, market segmentation, and the competitive positioning of a purpose-built AI billing tool against the in-house capabilities that firms like JLL are already building.

═══ SOURCE: Clear_Current_Interview_Summary_Marc_Spieler_NVIDIA.docx | FOLDER: Interview Summary Documents ═══
Interview Summary
Marc Spieler | NVIDIA
Senior Managing Director, Global Energy Industry
Conducted by Clayton Hester  |  April 3, 2026
1. Interviewee Profile
Marc Spieler has spent his entire career at the intersection of technology and energy. At NVIDIA, he leads business development, solution development, and go-to-market strategies that help energy companies enhance decision-making through HPC, visualization, data science, and AI. He serves on the Board of Directors of EPRI and on the Advisory Board of Grid Forward.
2. Marc's Three Go-to-Market Tracks
Marc's most valuable strategic contribution was identifying three distinct go-to-market tracks for Clear Current. He framed them as parallel strategies that address different buyer types.
Track 1: Direct to Enterprise (Current Approach)
Continue going to end customers with large, complex energy bills and auditing their bills directly. This is what Clear Current is doing today with its 12 pilots.
Track 2: Sell to Utilities
Go to utilities and offer the tool as a billing accuracy service. Marc identified two value propositions for the utility side: catching underbilling (which costs the utility lost revenue) and preventing overbilling (which generates customer service costs, regulatory risk, and potential legal exposure).
Utilities have whole teams managing commercial and industrial customers. Commercial and industrial customers account for 80% of power consumption. If the utility's billing system is producing systematic errors, they face regulatory risk from their state's utility commission.
Track 3: License as a Module to Billing Software & ERP Platforms
Marc's most expansive strategic recommendation: license Clear Current's AI auditing capability as a module embedded into existing billing software stacks and ERP systems - Oracle, SAP, Microsoft Dynamics, JD Edwards, and QuickBooks.
Implication for Clear Current: This removes the burden of direct sales from Clear Current - the ERP vendor becomes the distribution channel. Most enterprise bills flow through ERP systems already. If Clear Current's audit module sat inside that workflow, it would flag discrepancies before the bill is approved.
3. Pricing Model Intelligence
3.1 The Savings-Sharing Trap
Marc described a pattern he has seen repeatedly: a company agrees to take a percentage of savings found, but as soon as the savings become large, the client pushes to renegotiate to a fixed fee.
3.2 The Property Tax Consultant Analogy
Marc offered a directly relevant analogy: a property tax protest service that operates on an evergreen agreement with a 50% savings share. Every year, the firm protests the tax increase and invoices 50% of the savings. If they don't win, the customer pays nothing.
Implication for Clear Current: This model works because it's automated, recurring, low-friction, and the value is proven every cycle. Clear Current's billing audit could operate identically: every month, the system audits the bill, flags errors, and invoices a percentage of recovered savings. No savings, no charge.
3.3 The Proof Requirement
Key question Marc raised: Have the utilities actually recognized the mistakes and issued refunds? Clear Current needs case studies showing the full cycle: error identified, utility contacted, credit issued, dollars returned to customer.
4. The Bigger Market: Invoice Auditing Beyond Energy
One of Marc's most provocative insights was that Clear Current's technology is not limited to energy bills. He described a broader market for AI-powered invoice auditing:
Food service procurement companies buying from Sysco in huge quantities - did they get the right quantity, right price, right discounts?
Construction and building materials home builders receiving large lumber deliveries - are prices and discounts applied correctly?
Retail receipt auditing verifying coupons and discounts at the point of sale
Any negotiated-price procurement anywhere a company has negotiated rates and needs to verify invoices match those rates at volume
Implication for Clear Current: Marc is not suggesting Clear Current pivot away from energy. He's suggesting the technology has expansion potential beyond the energy vertical once the core product is proven. This expands the TAM story for investor conversations.
5. How Clear Current Can Enter the NVIDIA Ecosystem
5.1 The NVIDIA Inception Program
NVIDIA's Inception program includes over 40,000 startups globally, with approximately 700-800 in the energy space. The program is free, requires no equity, and provides co-marketing support, preferred pricing on NVIDIA hardware, and connections to the VC community.
Marc's requirement: Clear Current needs to demonstrate that it is using NVIDIA technology in its stack. If so, joining the program is a 'no-brainer' for co-marketing and enterprise introductions.
5.2 Cloud Provider Startup Programs
Marc advised Clear Current to identify which cloud provider they're running on (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud) and join that provider's startup program. Cloud providers do matchmaking between startups and enterprise customers.
5.3 NVIDIA's Internal Energy Purchasing
Kevin Yang handles sustainable purchasing at NVIDIA. If Clear Current is an Inception partner using NVIDIA technology, Marc could facilitate an introduction to have Clear Current audit NVIDIA's own energy bills as a reference customer.
6. Networking Leads & Introductions
Morgan Stacy, VP Connections & Strategic Accounts, National Grid Marc's primary referral. She previously served as VP of Business Development at Utilidata (NVIDIA's flagship smart meter AI partner), so she has experience on both the startup side and the utility side. Marc offered to make the introduction directly.
Kevin Yang, NVIDIA Internal contact for sustainable purchasing. Could connect Clear Current to NVIDIA's energy bills as a potential reference customer.
7. Strategic Recommendations for Dan & John
Prepared by Clayton Hester  |  CDL MBA Consulting Team
Team: Clayton Hester, Adam Case, Jason Wisnieski, Victor Pan
Clear Current Technologies  |  Dan Schreiber (CPO) & John Reuter (CEO)
Why This Interview Matters
Marc Spieler is not a user of energy management tools. He is the person at NVIDIA responsible for the company's entire global go-to-market strategy across oil and gas, renewables, power generation, and utilities. Named to Business Insider's 2024 AI Power List, he connects startups with enterprise energy buyers, manages NVIDIA's energy startup ecosystem (700-800 energy startups in the Inception program), and has direct relationships with Fortune 1000 energy companies and major utilities. This interview produced actionable GTM intelligence that no user interview could surface: three distinct go-to-market tracks, a channel strategy through ERP and billing software platforms, pricing model validation, and direct introductions to utility decision-makers.
Field
Detail
Name
Marc Spieler
Title
Senior Managing Director, Global Energy Industry
Company
NVIDIA
Location
Greater Houston
Tenure at NVIDIA
~6 years (since July 2019)
Prior Experience
Halliburton (~13 yrs): Director of Corporate Development, Global Customer Financial Services, Global R&D Outsourcing & Strategic Alliances | Silicon Graphics, Inc. (SGI): Business development across energy
Recognition
Business Insider 2024 AI Power List | Board of Directors, EPRI | Advisory Board, Grid Forward
Scope
NVIDIA's global go-to-market across oil & gas, renewables, power generation, and utilities
Ecosystem
Manages relationships with 700-800 energy startups in NVIDIA Inception program, plus Fortune 1000 energy companies
Marc's Advice on Track 1
Demonstrate that bills are consistently wrong, not just occasionally wrong. If errors are anomalies, customers will want a one-time audit. If errors are systematic and recurring, they'll want a subscription. 'It's one thing to say we've given you five years of billing. It wasn't an anomaly that they made a mistake - it was consistent. If the bills are consistently wrong, then a human can't possibly do this, and that means you want a subscription to your stuff.'
"You potentially go to a utility and said listen, take off the people's names. Let us audit 100 of your bills. We'll come back and tell you if they're inaccurate."
"The other go-to-market strategy would be to see if you could embed it into somebody else's billing thing. For $3 a month, we'll audit your electric bill. Now all of a sudden you guys just have to sell to QuickBooks and then QuickBooks sells to everybody else."
"As soon as they see you're on a trajectory to save them 20 million bucks, they're going to tell you 4 million bucks is way too expensive. They want to switch over to the fixed cost."
"Every year they send me an email that says, here's how much your taxes went up. We're gonna go protest it, and I agree to pay them 50% of the amount they saved me. If they don't win, I get nothing, they get nothing."
"The concept's great. The question is, how often are these bills wrong? If the answer is not very often, then you know. But based on what you're telling me, they're wrong a lot and you've had some big wins. My thing is go demonstrate those wins."
"So you solve this for electric billing, but who else has the most complex billing? Invoice auditing - that should be a great market for this."
Recommendation 1: Explore the Utility-Side Sales Motion
Marc identified a market angle Clear Current may not have fully considered: selling to utilities, not just enterprise energy buyers. If utility bills have errors in both directions, the utility has a financial and regulatory incentive to adopt an accuracy tool. Marc suggested approaching a utility like National Grid and offering to audit a sample of bills to demonstrate the error rate.
Recommendation 2: Pursue the ERP/Billing Software Licensing Strategy
Marc's most expansive strategic recommendation was licensing Clear Current's audit capability as a module inside ERP systems (SAP, Oracle, Microsoft Dynamics, QuickBooks, JD Edwards). If Clear Current's module sat inside that workflow, it would audit bills automatically before a human reviewer approves payment - removing the direct sales burden.
Recommendation 3: Confirm NVIDIA Technology Usage and Apply to Inception
Marc was explicit: if Clear Current is using NVIDIA technology in its stack (GPUs for cloud inference, any NVIDIA component), joining the Inception program is a 'no-brainer.' The program is free, requires no equity, and provides co-marketing support, VC introductions, and enterprise matchmaking.
Recommendation 4: Build the Full Recovery Case Study
Marc's most pointed question was whether utilities have actually issued refunds based on errors Clear Current found. If the answer is yes, that case study (error found, utility contacted, credit issued, dollars returned) is the single most powerful sales asset Clear Current can produce.
Recommendation 5: Connect with Morgan Stacy at National Grid
Marc offered to make a warm introduction to Morgan Stacy, VP of Connections & Strategic Accounts at National Grid. She has both startup experience (Utilidata) and utility-side authority. A conversation with her could validate or reshape the utility-side sales motion. Marc is making the introduction.
Recommendation 6: Frame the TAM Story Beyond Energy for Investors
Marc independently identified that Clear Current's technology applies beyond energy to any industry with complex, high-volume invoicing: food service procurement, construction materials, retail, and any negotiated-price B2B purchasing. Energy is the wedge; invoice auditing is the platform.
Recommendation 7: Investigate Utility Billing Software Vendors
Marc repeatedly returned to the idea that there are large billing software companies serving utilities - Oracle was named specifically. If Clear Current can license its audit module to the companies that build billing software for utilities, that creates a B2B2C channel.
