═══ SOURCE: Clear_Current_Interview_Summary_Phil_Combs_Trane.docx | FOLDER: Interview Summary Documents ═══
Interview Summary
Phil Combs | Trane Technologies
Sr. Digital Services Engineer, CMVP, CEM, PMVA
Conducted by Victor Pan  |  March 31, 2026
1. Interviewee Profile
Phil represents a rare combination of hands-on engineering depth and strategic technology perspective. His career arc from chiller testing through measurement & verification to digital services means he has touched every layer of the energy value chain. His recent move into the Senior Digital Services Engineer role (October 2025) means he is actively shaping how Trane deploys technology to its clients.
2. Current Tools & Methodology
Despite Trane's massive technology portfolio, the day-to-day energy analysis work is still done in Excel. The specialized software is only pulled out for guaranteed savings contracts that require statistical verification.
Excel spreadsheets the primary tool for day-to-day energy analysis. Phil structures data with consumption, demand, and cost columns, month-by-month rows, then uses charts and pivot tables for trend analysis.
Abraxas Option C specialized software for whole-facility energy measurement using linear regression. Used primarily for guaranteed energy performance contracting.
Time per meter approximately 10-15 minutes per meter to pull, enter, and review the appropriate data from a utility bill. For a client with 12 meters, that's roughly two hours of manual work per month on data collection alone.
3. Key Interview Findings
3.1 Bill Format Variation Is a Core Technical Challenge
Phil identified utility bill format variation as one of the most persistent challenges in energy analysis - and one that earlier AI tools have struggled with.
Phil expressed confidence that modern large language models would handle this variation much better:
Implication for Clear Current: Bill format adaptability is not a nice-to-have - it is the technical barrier that has prevented previous AI energy tools from gaining traction. If Clear Current's tariff and contract engine can reliably parse varied utility bill formats without manual configuration, that alone is a significant competitive advantage.
3.2 Regulatory Intelligence Is the Killer Feature Nobody Has
Phil's most enthusiastic and detailed feature request was regulatory intelligence - monitoring Public Utility Commission filings, utility board meetings, rate hike proposals, and grid operator decisions.
Implication for Clear Current: This is a feature no one in the competitive landscape currently offers well. If Clear Current can layer regulatory monitoring on top of billing intelligence, it creates a forward-looking product that goes beyond historical analysis. Phil described this as 'super powerful' - stronger language than he used for any other feature.
3.3 The Consumption vs. Cost Disconnect
Phil described a pattern that has become more pronounced in recent years: even when Trane helps clients reduce energy consumption, their costs continue to rise due to rate increases driven by data center demand, grid infrastructure investment, and electrification.
Implication for Clear Current: Clear Current's dashboard and reporting should be designed to show avoided cost, not just absolute cost reduction. The value story is 'you saved X relative to what you would have paid' - which requires both historical consumption analysis and rate trend intelligence.
3.4 Tariff Summarization & Rate Switch Recommendations
Phil identified utility tariffs as documents as complex as legal contracts, and specifically requested AI-powered tariff summarization and rate switch modeling.
Phil named Energy Toolbase as the closest existing competitor but noted the broader concept exceeds what Energy Toolbase currently offers.
Implication for Clear Current: Energy Toolbase is a named competitor for the tariff/rate optimization use case. Clear Current should conduct a detailed competitive teardown. Phil's assessment is that the combination of bill analysis + tariff intelligence + regulatory monitoring + chat interface exceeds what Energy Toolbase offers.
3.5 Internal AI Adoption Friction at Large Enterprises
One of the most candid moments in the interview came when Phil described the disconnect between Trane's external AI messaging and its internal AI adoption - a pattern he sees broadly across large enterprises.
Implication for Clear Current: When selling to large enterprises, IT and security teams will be a gate. Clear Current's enterprise sales motion needs to anticipate this: SOC 2 compliance, data handling documentation, security architecture white papers, and the ability to speak IT's language - not just the energy manager's.
3.6 Clients Are Firefighting, Not Strategizing
Implication for Clear Current: Clear Current cannot rely on users proactively logging in to discover insights. The dashboard must push alerts and the autonomous agent must surface issues without being asked. The 'Push to User' layer in Dan's UX architecture is not optional - it's essential for a customer base that is perpetually distracted by operational fires.
4. How Phil Would Use an AI Energy Management Tool
4.1 Desired Functions & Queries
"Go grab the last 24 months of electric data from this utility" automated data retrieval and structuring, eliminating 10-15 minutes per meter of manual collection
"Break it out month to month by cost, demand, consumption" structured time-series output ready for analysis
"Where do you see any statistically significant variation month to month?" automated anomaly detection with statistical rigor, not just threshold flagging
"What are your insights on why that happened?" root cause analysis: was it weather, a utility rate change, a special charge, or something internal?
"What's going on with this utility for the next six months?" forward-looking regulatory intelligence from PUC filings, board meetings, and grid operator decisions
"Which tariff rate is more beneficial for this customer?" rate switch analysis comparing general service vs. time-of-use based on actual usage profile
4.2 The Monetization Imperative
Phil was emphatic that technical energy analysis means nothing until it is translated into financial terms. Every insight Clear Current surfaces should be monetized by default.
5. Go-to-Market Intelligence
5.1 Who to Target Inside the Organization
Phil provided a clear hierarchy for who actually makes adoption decisions at enterprise customers, based on 20 years of selling into these organizations:
Energy or sustainability managers if the organization has one, start here. They are naturally interested and have domain knowledge.
Director of facilities motivated by cost savings and budget control. "That's what gets their attention."
Accounting / accounts payable anyone on the cost-control side managing utility spend.
CEOs and board members interested but will defer to the three groups above. They want internal champions to bring the recommendation.
5.2 Market Segmentation by Ease of Adoption
Easiest: Commercial office buildings, general companies without proprietary data concerns.
Moderate: MUSH markets (Municipalities, Universities, Schools, Hospitals) - interested but procurement processes apply.
Harder: Financial services, healthcare, and organizations with strict IT security.
Hardest: Federal government - extreme bureaucracy, multi-agency approval chains. Phil's advice: avoid for now.
6. Competitor & Tool Intelligence
7. Networking Leads from Phil
Erin Daley Trane corporate. Leader for measurement & verification engineering and guaranteed energy performance contracting. Phil described her as someone who would 'probably gain more traction' for any partnership conversation.
Casey Borst (casey.borst@trane.com) Trane corporate. Energy engineering and project development. Phil described her as having 'a lot of influence' on the corporate side.
Phil also offered to be an ongoing contact for follow-up questions and expressed strong interest in piloting the product when available.
8. Strategic Recommendations for Dan & John
Prepared by Clayton Hester  |  CDL MBA Consulting Team
Team: Clayton Hester, Adam Case, Jason Wisnieski, Victor Pan
Clear Current Technologies  |  Dan Schreiber (CPO) & John Reuter (CEO)
Why This Interview Matters
Phil Combs brings a perspective no other interviewee has provided: the measurement and verification engineer who has spent 7 years quantifying whether energy savings are real, and who now sits inside Trane's digital services organization. With nearly 20 years at Trane and 30+ years in HVAC, Phil has deep experience with the statistical methods that underpin energy performance contracting. His insights on bill format variation as a technical barrier, regulatory intelligence as a killer feature, and internal AI adoption friction at large enterprises are uniquely valuable for Clear Current's product and GTM strategy.
Field
Detail
Name
Phil Combs
Title
Senior Digital Services Engineer
Company
Trane Technologies
Location
Pueblo, Colorado Area (Pacific Southwest district)
Certifications
CMVP (Certified Measurement & Verification Professional), CEM (Certified Energy Manager), PMVA
Tenure at Trane
~20 years
Education
Oklahoma State University (BS Engineering, early 1990s)
Career at Trane
Chiller Test Engineer > Chiller Design & Reliability Engineer > M&V Engineer (7 yrs) > Renewable Energy & Power Dev. Engineer > Sr. Digital Services Engineer (since Oct 2025)
Key Markets
MUSH (Municipalities, Universities, Schools, Hospitals), federal facilities, commercial office buildings, data centers
"Utilities are still so varied and different. It can be frustrating because what they put on a bill and the format they put it on a bill, it can just vary. There's just basic things that anybody in our field should be able to go out and find and extract and pull real quick. But it's so different that [earlier AI tools] really stumbled with it."
"I have no doubts - as things have gone along now it's light years different in the capability to not only do the OCR part, but then the insights it'll get."
"If it could say, hey, I've also been looking at the publishings and the meetings of this utility's CEO and board - they're putting a price increase before the Public Utility Commission. Things like that are what really could be powerful because that saves somebody like me so much time."
"I can tell you six months from now this utility is actively talking to the Public Utility Commission. They're proposing this rate hike. This rate hike is going to mean this change in the cost of energy for you based on the last 12 months of operation. I think those are things that can be powerful."
"Even though we may be able to help them save energy, their costs have continued to go up. So one of the stories we help them explain is, yeah, your costs are going up, but if you did nothing, they'd go up even more - and by this amount."
"If it can summarize that - because if you've looked at them, it's like reading a legal document. Just get to the punch line. But then also have it make recommendations - for this customer, the utility just implemented a time-of-use rate, and based on the client's last 24 months of energy use, which one is more beneficial for them?"
"Trane on one hand goes out and is telling the world, 'hey, we can solve all your problems with our AI offerings' - but then on the other hand there's like me working internally, and they disable a lot of that stuff because they're like, 'well that's a security breach issue.'"
"A lot of clients unfortunately are firefighting - tyranny of the urgent. They're the people keeping the wheels on the car day-to-day. Even though there's the strategy that we always talk about, they're just a lot of times dealing with this piece of equipment broke, or tenants moving in or moving out."
"There's all this stuff where you go in and figure out how a facility or how equipment's using energy. But then it's like nobody cares until you monetize it. 'So what?' It's like, well, if you do this, here's your return on investment. Does that interest you?"
"If those three entities are in the CEO's and the board's ear, and they provide a compelling story of why, then the CEOs and the board will do it. But if you and I go and talk to them, maybe, but probably not."
Tool / Service
What It Does
Relevance to Clear Current
Energy Toolbase
Rate database, tariff modeling, rate switch analysis. Phil called it 'the closest' to what was described.
Named competitor for tariff intelligence. Clear Current's broader concept exceeds what it currently does.
Abraxas Option C
Whole-facility energy measurement using linear regression for guaranteed savings verification.
Not a direct competitor - a specialized M&V tool. Its regression methodology could inform CC's analytics.
Excel
The actual daily driver for energy professionals. Consumption, demand, cost columns; pivot tables; charts.
The real incumbent. CC needs to be faster and more insightful than a well-structured Excel workbook.
Recommendation 1: Build Regulatory Intelligence as a Differentiator
Phil's most enthusiastic feature request was forward-looking regulatory monitoring - PUC filings, rate hike proposals, grid operator decisions. No competitor does this well. If Clear Current can layer regulatory intelligence on top of billing analysis, it creates a product category that doesn't currently exist: predictive energy cost management.
Recommendation 2: Study Energy Toolbase as a Direct Competitor
Phil named Energy Toolbase as the closest existing product for rate database and tariff switch modeling. However, he assessed that the broader AI energy management concept exceeds Energy Toolbase's current capabilities. Clear Current should conduct a detailed competitive teardown.
Recommendation 3: Design for the Internal Champion, Not the CEO
Phil's 20 years of enterprise sales experience produced a clear GTM insight: CEOs don't adopt tools - they ratify decisions made by facilities directors, energy managers, and financial controllers. Clear Current should design outputs that are shareable and board-ready.
Recommendation 4: Monetize Every Insight by Default
Phil was emphatic: nobody cares about energy data until you put a dollar sign on it. Every alert, anomaly, and recommendation Clear Current surfaces should include a financial impact estimate. Not 'your demand spiked 15%' but 'your demand spike cost you $4,200 this month.'
Recommendation 5: Prepare Enterprise Security Documentation Early
Phil described a pattern where field-level employees want AI tools but IT departments block adoption. Clear Current needs SOC 2 compliance, security architecture documentation, and data handling policies ready before approaching large enterprises.
Recommendation 6: Excel Is the Real Competitor
Phil and his peers do most of their energy analysis in Excel spreadsheets. The threshold for adoption is: 'is this faster and more insightful than my well-structured Excel workbook?' Clear Current's chat interface needs to deliver insights that would take an expert 2+ hours to produce in Excel, in minutes.

Interview Summary
Randy Dawes | Hyatt Hotels Corporation
Director, Openings Engineering - Design & Construction Services
Conducted via Email  |  April 2026
1. Interviewee Profile
Randy's current title - Director of Openings Engineering - is focused on design and construction services for new Hyatt properties rather than day-to-day operational energy management. His prior role as Director of Hotel Engineering, Americas gave him deep operational experience, but his current vantage point is pre-opening and construction.
2. Hyatt Hotels - Structure & Energy Strategy Context
Hyatt Hotels Corporation operates across a mix of owned, managed, and franchised properties - a structure that has significant implications for how energy data is collected and who bears responsibility for energy bills. Because many Hyatt-branded properties are owned by third-party investors and operated under management agreements, the energy bills at those properties are ultimately the owner's responsibility, not Hyatt corporate's.
2.1 Hyatt's 2030 Environmental Goals (World of Care)
27.5% reduction in absolute Scope 1 and Scope 2 greenhouse gas emissions by 2030 vs. a 2019 baseline
53% reduction in Scope 3 emissions per square meter by 2030 vs. 2019
50% reduction in food waste per square meter at managed hotels by 2030 vs. 2019
Hyatt uses a proprietary internal tool called Hyatt EcoTrack to gather and analyze sustainability data from global hotels. Third-party data aggregators are used to pull consumption data directly from properties and feed it into the system of record.
3. Email Q&A - Verbatim Responses with Analysis
Q1: Biggest Pain Point - Data Visibility, Bill Accuracy, Rate Optimization, or Other?
Q2: Energy Management Calendar
Q3: If You Could Automate One Task, What Would It Be?
Q4: Billing Errors and Rate Discrepancies
4. Key Takeaways
Hyatt's owner-operator structure means corporate does not touch utility invoices. Billing error detection, rate optimization, and invoice processing happen at the property or ownership level, not at HQ.
The biggest pain Randy surfaced is metrics fragmentation: different units across properties make it hard to communicate energy consumption in a way that means something to the person using the energy.
Hyatt's energy calendar is an ESG reporting calendar, not an operational one. Q1 prior-year reporting and a mid-year check-in are the two anchor points.
The right buyer in hospitality is not a brand's corporate team. It's individual hotel owners, asset managers, or management companies running multiple properties who bear direct responsibility for utility costs.
Invoice automation (solving late arrivals and associated late fees) is the most clearly articulated problem Randy has seen at the operational level, consistent with what Roger Goldstein described at Panda.
5. Strategic Implications
Prepared by Clayton Hester  |  CDL MBA Consulting Team
Team: Clayton Hester, Adam Case, Jason Wisnieski, Victor Pan
Clear Current Technologies  |  Dan Schreiber (CPO) & John Reuter (CEO)
Why This Interview Matters
Randy Dawes brings a perspective not yet represented in this research program: the hospitality industry, and specifically the unique owner-operator structure of a global hotel brand. Hyatt operates more than 1,450 properties across 80+ countries through a mix of owned, managed, and franchised hotels. This means corporate-level energy strategy and property-level energy execution are often handled by entirely different parties, creating a data collection and accountability gap that is structurally different from what any prior interviewee has described.
Note on Interview Format: This engagement was conducted entirely over email. As a result, responses were shorter than those gathered in live interviews with other participants in this research program. Insights are documented verbatim where possible, with analysis provided to add context. Gaps in coverage are flagged explicitly throughout the document.
Field
Detail
Name
Randy Dawes
Title
Director, Openings Engineering - Design & Construction Services
Company
Hyatt Hotels Corporation
Location
Greater Chicago Area (Hybrid)
Contact
randy.dawes@hyatt.com
Education
University of New Orleans (1981-1984); Delgado Community College (AAS)
Tenure at Hyatt (current)
January 2024 - Present (~2 years)
Prior Experience
VP of Facilities, Enlivant (senior living operator) - 7 years | Director of Hotel Engineering, Americas, Hyatt - 11 years | Director of Engineering, Hyatt Regency New Orleans - ~4 years
Career Overview
40+ year career in facilities and engineering management across hospitality and senior living.
"Given the structure of Hyatt, we have multiple partners within the operators and owners of hotels. With the magnitude of these differences the collection of the data is listed as a requirement to each hotel. How that is achieved is the use of third parties that can access the data from the hotel and then supply that information into the system of record for our energy and water website."
Analysis
Randy did not directly answer which pain point is biggest. His response describes the data collection mechanism rather than the pain. The implication is that data collection itself - getting reliable consumption data out of hundreds of independently operated properties - is the underlying challenge. The phrase 'multiple partners within operators and owners' signals a fragmented accountability structure. No single party owns the full picture. A follow-up question should ask: where does data go missing or arrive inconsistently, and what does that cost in terms of reporting accuracy?
"The company is focused on year 2030 goals for reduction of consumption. Our constant focus is on those reductions and how to contribute to a better world. Our reporting data is required at the end of the 1st quarter for previous year along with a mid-year check in on progress."
Analysis
The calendar described is an ESG reporting calendar, not an operational energy management calendar. End of Q1 = prior-year reporting deadline. Mid-year = progress check-in. This is the clearest signal yet that Randy's perspective is corporate/strategic, not property-operational. The seasonal consumption patterns, procurement windows, and reactive stress points that characterize operators like Panda or Texas State simply don't appear at this level. For the Seasonal Energy Management Calendar deliverable, Hyatt's profile is: Q1 reporting deadline, mid-year check-in, otherwise continuous focus on 2030 goal trajectory.
"The energy management wish would be better reporting of consumption; the world today has many different metrics for measuring these critical items. Thinking how can we get everyone on the same platform for these metrics, similarity would allow for easier understanding of what each unit of water means, and what each unit of energy means. With the multiple different recording or reporting units used today, transferring that data is easy to do for the corporation, but difficult to show what each hotel's consumption means to the person who consumes the unit."
Analysis
This is the most substantive and differentiated finding from this interview. Randy is describing a metrics standardization problem: different properties report energy in different units, making it impossible to create a common language around consumption that resonates at the property level. The distinction he draws is important: data transfer to corporate is manageable, but making that data meaningful to the person actually consuming the energy is not solved. For Clear Current: a product that normalizes energy data across properties and surfaces it in terms that resonate with hotel operators (cost per occupied room, energy intensity per square foot) addresses this pain directly.
"At the corporate level we do not review these or get access to that. In my previous life there are more issues with the invoice arriving late or at the due date, this seems to be a concern based on the billing of late fees. Automation of invoices appears to be a fix to that issue."
Analysis
Randy confirmed directly that billing error detection does not happen at the Hyatt corporate level. His comment about 'previous life' (likely his Enlivant VP role overseeing senior living facilities) is notable: late invoices and late fees were a real operational pain there. He sees invoice automation as the fix - which aligns with what Roger at Panda described as the primary function of NG Insight. For Clear Current: Hyatt corporate is not the right buyer for billing error detection. The right entry point in hospitality is either (a) individual hotel owners/operators managing their own properties, or (b) hotel management companies running portfolios of managed properties.
Finding 1: Hospitality Corporate Is Not the Right Entry Point
Hyatt corporate does not touch invoices. For this vertical, Clear Current should target individual hotel owners, asset managers, or management companies running portfolios of managed properties rather than corporate sustainability teams.
Finding 2: Metrics Normalization Is a Hospitality-Specific Need
Randy's most substantive finding was that different properties report energy in different units, making portfolio comparison impossible. A product that normalizes energy data and surfaces it in operator-meaningful terms (cost per occupied room, energy intensity per sq ft) addresses a real gap.
Finding 3: Invoice Automation Is the Hospitality Entry Pitch
At the operational level, the clearest pain Randy described is invoice processing: late arrivals and late fees. The entry pitch for hospitality operators should lead with 'we make sure your bills arrive, get paid on time, and are correct' before moving to optimization.
Finding 4: Follow Up Is Recommended Before Drawing Firm Conclusions
This engagement was conducted via email with limited response depth. Randy's perspective reflects corporate/strategic viewpoint rather than property operations. A follow-up interview with a hotel GM, director of engineering, or hotel management company representative would provide the operational perspective needed to fully map the hospitality vertical.

═══ SOURCE: Clear_Current_Interview_Summary_Roger_Goldstein_Panda.docx | FOLDER: Interview Summary Documents ═══
Interview Summary
Roger Goldstein | Panda Restaurant Group
Executive Director, Facilities & Energy
Conducted by Clayton Hester  |  April 2026
1. Interviewee Profile
Roger's path to energy management was not linear. He built his career in facilities, working his way from regional manager to running Panda's entire facilities organization before taking on energy and waste in 2014. His perspective on energy is grounded in operational reality, not engineering theory. He thinks in terms of business impact, dollar leakage, and vendor relationships.
2. Panda Restaurant Group - Energy Operations Overview
2.1 Third-Party Bill Processing: NG Insight
Panda outsources its utility bill processing entirely to NG Insight, a third-party bill pay service. All invoices are routed to NG Insight, where they are received electronically or manually scanned, keyed into the system, compiled by due date, and batched back to Panda for a single consolidated payment run once per day. Without this, Roger estimated it would require four to six dedicated accounting headcount just to manage bill intake.
2.2 Exception Monitoring is Manual and Threshold-Based
NG Insight allows Panda to set alert parameters. If a location's bill spikes significantly above its rolling average (Roger's example: a $2,000 average bill jumps to $12,000), the system flags it as an exception. The actual review and triage is handled by a small internal team: one energy manager and one energy associate. Data extracted from NG Insight is pulled to Excel, where the team scrubs it for anomalies and monitors year-over-year spend trends.
3. Key Interview Findings
3.1 Billing Errors Persist Because Averages Mask Them
One of the most important findings from this call is that Panda's current monitoring approach is fundamentally average-based, which means slow-burn errors can go undetected for extended periods.
Roger's most vivid example: a single meter was discovered to be covering two outlets in a shared tenant space. For an entire year, Panda had been paying the neighbor's electricity. The error was not flagged by NG Insight's threshold system because the charges had been consistent enough to establish a new baseline.
Implication for Clear Current: This is the clearest real-world validation of the 'baseline drift' problem in multi-site energy management. Threshold-based exception rules work for spikes. They don't catch gradual or systematic errors that blend into the average over time. Clear Current's anomaly detection should be framed not just as 'spike detection' but as 'pattern deviation detection.'
3.2 Bill Management Is the Operational Foundation, Not an Afterthought
He also described a second, less-discussed risk: planned utility outages. When a utility company schedules maintenance that would take out power mid-business day, Panda's energy team actively negotiates with the utility to reschedule to off-hours or off-days.
Implication for Clear Current: The core value proposition of 'we pay the right bill on time' resonates immediately with a buyer like Roger. The error-catching layer is additive. The entry pitch for QSR buyers should lead with operational risk reduction (shutoffs, missed bills, wrong charges) before moving to optimization.
3.3 Water Anomaly Detection Is Already Solved, But Electricity Isn't
Panda has deployed a product called H2NO by Conservation Labs across locations to monitor water usage in real time, 24 hours a day. The driver was a $32,000 water bill caused by an undetected underground irrigation leak. Real-time monitoring solved the problem. The electricity equivalent of this infrastructure does not exist in the same way.
Implication for Clear Current: H2NO provides a mental model Roger already trusts: real-time monitoring that surfaces a problem before the bill arrives. Clear Current should be framed in the same terms for electricity and gas: 'What H2NO did for water, we do for your entire utility bill stack.'
3.4 HVAC Aging is the Biggest Dollar Leak in Restaurants
Implication for Clear Current: HVAC degradation creates a billing signal: electricity usage climbs gradually at a specific location even when headcount, hours, and weather are controlled for. A billing intelligence tool that flags this pattern location by location could help energy managers dispatch maintenance before the unit fails completely.
3.5 Energy Calendar: Electricity Peaks Summer, Gas is Flat
Roger confirmed the restaurant industry energy calendar is straightforward. Electricity peaks from June through September, driven almost entirely by air conditioning load. Gas is used primarily for cooking and remains relatively stable year-round, with a modest winter uptick. There is no complex demand management calendar equivalent to what higher education institutions manage.
Implication for Clear Current: Summer = maximum electricity exposure for QSR operators. This is the window when rate optimization, demand charge monitoring, and anomaly detection generate the most dollar value. Product positioning for the QSR vertical should align feature demos with peak exposure periods.
4. How Roger Would Use an AI Energy Management Tool
Exception upgrading: Moving beyond threshold-based flags to AI-detected pattern deviations that a static average would miss
Baseline drift detection: Identifying locations where the 'normal' has shifted upward due to an undetected error or equipment issue
Location-level HVAC fingerprinting: Flagging stores where electricity usage climbs beyond weather-normalized expectations, suggesting aging or failing HVAC
Multi-utility correlation: Connecting water anomalies (already monitored by H2NO) with electricity and gas anomalies for a unified picture
Savings documentation: Producing clear dollar-impact reports that justify the tool's cost to finance leadership
5. Key Takeaways for the QSR Vertical
At 2,600 locations and ~10,000 bills/month, manual bill review is not feasible. The entire operation runs through a third-party processor (NG Insight) with threshold-based exception rules.
The threshold system catches spikes but misses baseline drift - the gradual accumulation of systematic errors. This is Clear Current's primary competitive opening.
The H2NO water monitoring product is the mental model to borrow. Roger already trusts the concept of real-time anomaly detection. Clear Current needs to be that for electricity and gas.
HVAC aging is the #1 dollar leak at the location level. If Clear Current can correlate billing anomalies with likely HVAC degradation, that is a concrete, actionable alert for facilities teams.
NG Insight is a potential channel partner. Roger offered to connect the team with his account lead. Multiple interviewees have now named NG Insight as a relevant player.
6. Strategic Recommendations for Dan & John
Prepared by Clayton Hester  |  CDL MBA Consulting Team
Team: Clayton Hester, Adam Case, Jason Wisnieski, Victor Pan
Clear Current Technologies  |  Dan Schreiber (CPO) & John Reuter (CEO)
Why This Interview Matters
Roger Goldstein is one of the most operationally relevant interviewees in this research program. As Executive Director of Facilities and Energy at Panda Restaurant Group, he holds direct oversight of energy management, utility billing, and facilities operations across approximately 2,600 locations in the U.S. He has been at Panda for nearly 20 years and took on the energy portfolio in 2014 after building out the entire facilities organization from scratch. He sits at the exact intersection of scale, operational complexity, and dollar-driven decision-making that Clear Current is designed to serve. Roger is the first true multi-site commercial end-user in this research program.
Field
Detail
Name
Roger Goldstein
Title
Executive Director, Facilities & Energy
Company
Panda Restaurant Group
Location
Los Angeles, CA (Southern California)
Certifications
CRFP (Certified Restaurant Facilities Professional)
Industry Involvement
RFMA (Restaurant Facilities Managers Association) Board of Directors Member
Education
BS, Range Science & Ranch Management, Texas A&M University (Class of 1983)
Tenure at Panda
~20 years (started 2006)
Prior Experience
Ranch management | Banking & financial planning | Construction | Hotel management | Restaurant equipment & refrigeration service (10 yrs, Albuquerque)
Career at Panda
Regional Facilities Manager (2006) > Supervising Facilities Leader (2008) > Director of Facilities (2011) > Executive Director, Facilities (2013) > Added Energy & Waste portfolio (2014)
Scale of Oversight
~2,600 Panda Express locations across the U.S., approximately 10,000 utility bills processed per month
Key Takeaway for Clear Current
NG Insight is already named by multiple interviewees (including Roger at Panda and the pending ENGIE contact) as a direct go-to-market pathway. Roger proactively offered to connect the team with his NG Insight account lead. This is a warm channel introduction opportunity that should be followed up on immediately.
Key Takeaway for Clear Current
The current workflow is: bill processing by a third party > threshold-based exception flagging > manual review by two people using Excel. This is exactly the workflow Clear Current is designed to improve. The exception logic is rule-based and static. Clear Current's AI layer could identify patterns that a static threshold would miss entirely.
"It does happen. It can go on for a while and then accounting or operations will catch something like that. It's like, hey, how come I haven't had any electric charges on my P&L for six months, or why were the last four months so expensive?"
"The most common cause of a shutoff or a disruption in service is a bill got overlooked and didn't get paid. And it's always a mistake. Somebody just missed it, lost it, bad address, keyed it in wrong to the system."
"You can have an air conditioner that has a mechanical issue and it's failing, barely producing any cold air, but it's not noticed for many days or weeks. You're just pouring all that electricity into that unit and not getting any real value from it."
Recommendation 1: Frame the Value Proposition Around 'Pattern Deviation,' Not Just 'Spike Detection'
Roger's billing error stories all share a common thread: the problem blended into the baseline before being caught. Clear Current's AI layer should be explicitly positioned as catching what threshold rules can't - systematic drift, gradual creep, and errors that become the new normal before anyone notices.
Recommendation 2: Lead QSR Pitches with Operational Risk, Then Optimization
Roger's most visceral concern is utility shutoffs and missed bills - not rate optimization. The entry pitch should establish that Clear Current prevents the operational nightmare of a shutoff, then layer in the savings opportunity. Get the risk conversation first; the ROI conversation follows naturally.
Recommendation 3: Borrow the H2NO Mental Model in QSR Sales Materials
Roger already trusts and values real-time anomaly detection for water because H2NO proved the concept. Clear Current should explicitly reference this model in conversations with QSR buyers: 'What H2NO did for your water, we do for your entire utility stack.' This shortcuts the 'prove it works' skepticism.
Recommendation 4: Pursue the NG Insight Channel Introduction
Roger offered to connect the team with his NG Insight account lead. NG Insight processes bills for a significant portion of the QSR and multi-site retail market. If Clear Current's AI layer can be offered as an enhancement to NG Insight's exception monitoring, that is a channel partnership that provides access to NG Insight's entire customer base.
Recommendation 5: Build an HVAC Degradation Alert into the Product Roadmap
HVAC is the #1 electricity consumer in restaurants. Degradation is gradual, invisible, and expensive. If Clear Current can flag location-level electricity usage creep that correlates with likely HVAC degradation - especially in summer months - that is a high-value, differentiated alert that facility managers and energy executives would act on immediately.

═══ SOURCE: Clear_Current_Interview_Summary_Scott_Czubkowski_Ascension (1).docx | FOLDER: Interview Summary Documents ═══
Interview Summary
Scott Czubkowski, PE, CHC, CEM | Medxcel (Ascension Health)
National Director, Energy & Facility Performance
Conducted by Clayton Hester  |  April 14, 2026
1. Interviewee Profile
Scott built his career at the intersection of engineering, business development, and energy systems — starting with nuclear propulsion plant testing on 688-class fast attack submarines, then building automation and energy services at Siemens across three continents, then healthcare facility performance. His multi-decade experience means he has seen technology cycles come and go in the energy management space, which makes his skepticism about AI labeling and his enthusiasm for specific capabilities equally well-grounded.
2. Ascension / Medxcel — Energy Operations Overview
2.1 Organizational Structure of the Former Energy Team
Scott described the team he built and ran for five and a half years as structured around three specialized groups:
Supply Side Team (4 people): Everything upstream of the meter — utility bill management, tariffs, solar feasibility, microgrids, natural gas procurement. Responsible for $265M annual utility spend and hedging strategy.
Engineering Team: Downstream of the meter — energy efficiency projects, building automation controls, electrification of heating systems, energy recovery systems, chiller optimization.
Facilities Performance Team: Works directly with hospital operators to train staff on equipment operation, remove the human-error element, and drive behavioral efficiency. Scott noted this team's training investments produced 11% energy savings vs. 4% from capital equipment replacements — at a fraction of the cost.
2.2 Billing Infrastructure: Peer AI (Constellation Energy)
Ascension transitioned to Peer AI — an AI-powered energy analytics platform owned by Constellation Energy — approximately nine months before this interview, replacing a prior third-party processor they had been unhappy with. Peer AI handles bill receipt, payment, anomaly flagging, and cross-portfolio analytics.
Scott's Peer AI use cases in practice:
Dead meter detection: Peer AI identified a location paying $32/month, exactly the same amount, for months — flagging it as a dead meter nobody had turned off. Without pattern-matching across time, this never would have been caught.
Building comparisons: Using square footage and geographic data, Peer AI benchmarks similar hospitals against each other and flags buildings that are consuming disproportionately for their size and climate zone.
Polar vortex nomination management: Scott's most compelling ROI story: Peer AI is being used to monitor weather forecasts and alert the natural gas trading desk 24 hours before extreme cold events, allowing Ascension to increase gas nominations before prices spike. One polar vortex event (likely Winter Storm Uri) cost $7.2 million in three days. This program is estimated to be worth several million dollars annually.
3. Key Interview Findings
3.1 The Dead Meter Problem — Ascension's Largest Billing Error Category
When asked where he finds the most frequent billing errors, Scott's answer was immediate and specific: paying for sites Ascension no longer occupies.
With over 2,500 care sites, Ascension constantly moves in and out of leased spaces, medical office buildings, and strip mall locations. When a site is vacated, the meter is not always turned off — and if the baseline is consistent, the threshold-based exception system doesn't flag it. Peer AI's business case was partially justified on this category alone: $1 million/year in savings from detecting and eliminating payments for vacated sites.
Implication for Clear Current: This mirrors Roger Goldstein's 'paying the neighbor's electricity' story at Panda, but at healthcare scale. For multi-site operators who frequently open and close locations — QSRs, retailers, health systems, banks — this 'ghost meter' category is a recurring, predictable, and high-value error class. Clear Current should build a specific 'ghost meter detection' alert type and quantify it in sales conversations.
3.2 The Layman's Interface Is the Product
When asked what he would build if starting an AI energy management platform from scratch, Scott's answer was unambiguous: make it accessible to non-technical administrators.
He described a specific recurring pain point with hospital administration: 'Why is my utility bill not matching my budget?' That question — not demand optimization or tariff analysis — is the conversation Scott has with finance leadership constantly. An AI system that can answer that question in natural language, without requiring a technical filter, is the product he needs.
Implication for Clear Current: This is the strongest validation yet of Dan's dual-audience UX architecture. Scott confirmed that the finance/administration audience can't access or interpret technical energy data — but the CEO picking up his cell phone and asking 'what is the cost of my utilities in St. John's in Tulsa, Oklahoma' is exactly what Peer AI has enabled, and exactly what Clear Current should replicate. The chat interface is not a nice-to-have for this vertical — it is the product.
3.3 Rules-Based vs. True AI: Scott's Skepticism
Scott made a distinction that several other interviewees touched on but he articulated most precisely: the difference between rules-based systems (which he acknowledged are highly effective) and true AI (which he defined as a system that writes its own algorithms rather than running pre-defined rules).
He used this framework to describe fault detection technology (FTD) — a category of software that monitors building systems for anomalies. He noted that FTD systems are effective and proven, but they are rule-based, not AI. This distinction matters to him because it affects how much he trusts a platform's claims about what it can do.
Implication for Clear Current: Clear Current should be precise about what its system does and doesn't do when engaging sophisticated buyers like Scott. The tariff logic and billing reconstruction capabilities are genuinely AI-powered, while the anomaly threshold alerts are more rules-adjacent. Being accurate about this distinction will build trust faster than overselling.
3.4 Data Security and Vendor Transitions
Scott described a PricewaterhouseCoopers engagement from roughly five years ago in which Ascension turned over data for 125 hospitals with over 12 years of operational data — potentially the largest acute-care hospital database in existence. He described fighting hard to anonymize it while still getting the analysis value.
He also noted that the AI/energy software market moves so fast that pilot programs often hit the market too late: 'By the time we got it on the system, six months later I was talking about it, people were like, what are you talking about? This is the name of our company now.'
Implication for Clear Current: Two clear product implications: (1) Data security documentation — who can see the data, how is it anonymized, what are the data handling obligations — needs to be ready before any enterprise healthcare conversation, as Scott raised it unprompted. (2) The sales cycle in healthcare energy is long, and the market changes fast enough that vendor identity can shift during a pilot. Clear Current should be building customer relationships around outcomes (dollars found, recovered, avoided), not software features that may look different six months from now.
3.5 The Training vs. Capital Investment Insight
Scott offered an unexpected insight about the relative ROI of different energy investment types. Over his tenure at Ascension, capital equipment replacements (new HVAC, lighting, controls) produced about 4% energy savings across the portfolio — representing tens of millions of dollars in project spend. Meanwhile, the Facilities Performance team's work training hospital operators to run their equipment correctly produced 11% savings — at a fraction of the capital cost.
Implication for Clear Current: This is a counterintuitive finding for energy technology vendors: the biggest efficiency gains often come from changing how people operate existing equipment, not from buying new equipment or software. Clear Current's platform should think about how it can support the behavioral/operational side — for example, flagging when a building's consumption profile suggests equipment is not being operated as scheduled, or when consumption is inconsistent with building occupancy patterns.
4. Annual Energy Management Calendar — Ascension/Medxcel
Scott confirmed the healthcare calendar follows the seasonal heating/cooling cycle described by other interviewees. He added two healthcare-specific dimensions:
Budget forecasting: Occurs during each organization's fiscal year cycle. AI that could accelerate and improve the accuracy of utility budget forecasting was one of Scott's explicit requests — 'one thing AI could help with is budgeting going much quicker.'
Hedging windows: Natural gas hedging decisions are most effectively made when markets are favorable (typically not mid-winter). Having real-time consumption data alongside market outlooks is what makes the Peer AI polar vortex nomination program valuable.
5. Strategic Recommendations for Dan & John
Prepared by Clayton Hester  |  CDL MBA Consulting Team
Team: Clayton Hester, Adam Case, Jason Wisnieski, Victor Pan
Clear Current Technologies  |  Dan Schreiber (CPO) & John Reuter (CEO)
Why This Interview Matters
Scott Czubkowski is the National Director of Energy & Facility Performance at Medxcel, the facilities management subsidiary of Ascension Health. He held the equivalent energy management role directly at Ascension for five and a half years before energy functions were reorganized into a new internal resource group in December 2025. He is one of the most credentialed energy managers in the healthcare industry — PE, CHC, CEM, licensed contractor in Florida, adjunct professor, conference speaker — and brings a career spanning nuclear submarine engineering, Siemens business development across the U.S., Canada, and Switzerland, and over a decade of healthcare energy program leadership. His current oversight covers roughly 100 hospitals and 2,500 care sites with a $265 million annual utility spend. He is the clearest institutional voice in this research program on what works, what doesn't, and what the next generation of energy AI platforms needs to do to earn trust at this scale.

Scale
~100 hospitals, 2,500+ care sites; ~$265M annual utility spend; ~1.9 TWh annual energy management

"Paying for sites that we no longer own. I mean, we've paid meters for over 2 years with different people."
"All the technology is out there to control things, do things, monitor things, measure things, whatever. It's how I get administrators to interact with the system without being technical, right? Is my building using more energy than a sister building? What would be my projected utility bill for next month based on current weather outlook?"
"Is it going to help us mitigate cost? Absolutely, because it's going to look at a bunch of different things. But I'm one of those guys that still think some AI things are strictly rules-based."
"I've got $35 million of savings. Well, remember, to do the $35 million of savings, I spend 5x on the capital side compared to the training side, yet I got more out of this side."
Recommendation 1: Build a Ghost Meter Detection Alert as a Named Feature
Scott's most common billing error category — paying for vacated sites — is a pattern-detection problem, not a spike alert problem. A 'ghost meter' detection module that flags when a site shows consistent low-level payments with no corresponding operational activity (no HVAC cycling, no occupancy correlation, no lease in system) would be directly valued by health systems, large retailers, and QSR operators alike.
Recommendation 2: The Natural Language Executive Query Is the Healthcare Sales Demo
Scott described Peer AI's most-used feature as the ability for a non-technical executive to ask in plain language about utility costs at a specific hospital. 'What is the cost of my utilities in St. John's in Tulsa' is a sentence that sells the product. Clear Current's demo for hospital administration should start here — not with an anomaly chart or a tariff comparison, but with the moment an executive asks a simple question and gets a real answer without calling an engineer.
Recommendation 3: Prepare Data Security Documentation Before Any Ascension/Medxcel Conversation
Scott mentioned data security unprompted and noted Ascension's history of fighting to protect data when working with third parties. SOC 2 compliance, data anonymization protocols, and a clear statement of what data Clear Current accesses vs. what stays on-premise are required reading before any follow-up conversation with this organization.
Recommendation 4: Reference the Constellation / Peer AI Relationship When Positioning Against Incumbents
Peer AI (Constellation Energy) is a named incumbent at Ascension. Understanding what Peer AI does and doesn't do — and where its gaps are — is important for any follow-up engagement. Scott noted Ascension has only been on Peer AI nine months and is still 'scratching the surface.' There may be a product gap conversation worth having as their use deepens.

═══ SOURCE: Clear_Current_Interview_Summary_Sean_Sevy_Houston_Methodist (1).docx | FOLDER: Interview Summary Documents ═══
Interview Summary
Sean Sevy | Houston Methodist
Director of Operations, System Facilities
Conducted by Clayton Hester  |  April 14, 2026
1. Interviewee Profile
Sean's career has been spent entirely within Houston healthcare systems, which gives him a Texas-specific perspective on energy management that is distinct from the Midwest and Mountain West systems interviewed elsewhere in this research program. He began his facilities career in 1990 when energy management was not yet a strategic priority — his description of the pneumatic-to-digital-to-AI transition over 35 years is the best historical arc captured in this research program.
2. Houston Methodist — Energy Operations Overview
8 hospitals + 300+ outpatient and specialty locations across Greater Houston
Energy management focus: building automation systems, demand charge management, power factor correction, benchmarking via Energy Star
Energy Print (third-party platform): used for utility bill tracking, Energy Star score maintenance, and benchmarking against peer hospital systems; does not integrate with building automation
Prism Energy (broker): manages energy procurement contracts and provides cursory invoice review for calculation errors and tariff compliance
Next step: considering building automation-integrated platforms that combine operational HVAC intelligence with utility billing data — Sean described this as likely 3-5 years out for widespread adoption
3. Key Interview Findings
3.1 The 35-Year Arc: From Pneumatics to AI
Sean provided the most historically grounded perspective on energy management evolution of any interviewee in this research program. He described four distinct eras:
Pre-1990s: Energy was cheap. Comfort, not efficiency, was the priority. All controls were manual or pneumatic. No strategic focus on energy spend.
1990s: Pneumatic to direct digital control (DDC) transition. Energy savings became a secondary benefit of better control — automation enabled turning systems off and on on schedule. Simple payback calculations introduced. Lighting retrofits (T12 to T8 fluorescent) became standard.
2000s-2010s: Building automation systems (BAS) matured. Demand side management emerged — monitoring 15-minute demand peaks, installing capacitors for power factor improvement. LED lighting. Energy Star program adopted.
Current and emerging: AI components being added to BAS control loops — self-learning control optimization. Material modeling for construction specifications. AI-powered benchmarking through third-party platforms. Early stages of AI integration with building operations data.
Implication for Clear Current: This historical arc is valuable context for positioning Clear Current's platform. The billing intelligence layer Sean is currently using (Energy Print) operates entirely outside the building — it sees only what the utility bills for. The next frontier he anticipates is a layer that bridges billing data and building operations data. Clear Current is positioned at the billing intelligence layer, which is where Sean currently lives. The question for future product development is whether Clear Current can begin to bridge toward the building operations layer.
3.2 Energy Print — What It Does and Doesn't Do
Sean is an active user of Energy Print, a third-party energy analytics platform he described as primarily used for benchmarking and bill tracking. He was candid about both its value and its gaps.
What Energy Print does well:
Benchmarking: Compares Houston Methodist hospitals against peer hospitals in Energy Star and helps identify which facilities are performing below expectations for their size and type.
Bill tracking and Energy Star integration: Receives utility data, feeds it into Energy Star for compliance, and surfaces year-over-year trend comparisons.
Budgeting support: Provides data inputs for annual utility budget projections.
What Energy Print does not do well, in Sean's words:
Sean's specific gap: Energy Print's forecasting models assume a relatively stable building portfolio. Houston Methodist adds campuses, hospital wings, and outpatient facilities regularly. Standard trend-based forecasting doesn't account for growth, and the platform has no way to model 'what if we added X square feet of this type of care.'
Implication for Clear Current: This is a concrete product gap with a specific customer request attached: growth modeling for energy forecasting. Healthcare systems that are actively expanding — and there are many — need a forecasting tool that can absorb new building parameters and project what their utility spend will look like before the first bill arrives. This is not a standard feature of any current platform. If Clear Current can model forward-looking scenarios that include expansion parameters, that is a genuine differentiation for high-growth health systems.
3.3 The Billing Error Landscape at Houston Methodist
Sean described billing errors as 'minimal' on their invoices — a notably different profile from what other interviewees described. He attributed this to Prism Energy's broker oversight providing a cursory calculation review. However, he was actively working on one case: a campus showing a power factor of 79-81%, far below the typical 93-94% baseline for modern equipment.
Sean described this as a suspected meter malfunction rather than an actual power factor problem — the building's equipment is too modern to have that low a power factor, suggesting the meter is misreporting.
Implication for Clear Current: This is a specific, named feature validation: power factor monitoring from billing data is detectable without building automation integration. Don Johnson raised power factor as his first unprompted feature request from Trane's perspective. Sean Sevy is actively investigating what he suspects is a power factor meter error. Clear Current should confirm whether its tariff analysis engine can detect power factor anomalies from billing data alone — if so, this is a clear quick-win alert type with healthcare validation.
3.4 Demand Charge Management — Houston Methodist's Longstanding Focus
Sean described demand charge management as a strategic priority that Houston Methodist has focused on since the 1990s. The 15-minute peak demand window during summer months is the key billing variable that drives capacity charges — and controlling it has been a primary motivation for BAS investment, power factor correction, and load scheduling.
He also confirmed the AI self-learning control loop trend: modern BAS vendors are adding AI that optimizes control loops in real time, learning from weather, occupancy, and equipment state data to minimize demand peaks without compromising comfort. This is separate from billing intelligence — it is embedded in the building controls layer.
Implication for Clear Current: Houston Methodist represents a sophisticated buyer who is already pursuing demand management actively through building controls. For Clear Current, the pitch here is not demand reduction — it's billing verification: 'your BAS is working to lower your demand. We make sure the utility is calculating your demand charge correctly based on what your meters actually recorded.'
5. Annual Energy Management Calendar — Houston Methodist
Sean described the calendar as consistent with other hospital systems: summer is peak electricity from cooling, winter is peak natural gas from heating, shoulder seasons are optimization windows. Key Houston-specific observations:
Houston climate: The summer cooling season is extended and intense relative to most other U.S. healthcare markets. Demand charge risk is highest June-September.
Growth-driven complexity: Regular campus expansion means budget forecasting is complicated by new facility openings — a gap he identified as a weakness in Energy Print.
CenterPoint relationship: Houston Methodist works with CenterPoint Energy as its primary utility. Sean noted calculation errors with CenterPoint have improved over time, though the broker Prism Energy provides oversight.
5. Strategic Recommendations for Dan & John
Prepared by Clayton Hester  |  CDL MBA Consulting Team
Team: Clayton Hester, Adam Case, Jason Wisnieski, Victor Pan
Clear Current Technologies  |  Dan Schreiber (CPO) & John Reuter (CEO)
Why This Interview Matters
Sean Sevy is the Director of Operations for System Facilities at Houston Methodist, one of the nation's top-ranked hospital systems with eight hospitals and 300+ locations across the greater Houston area. He has spent nearly 23 years at Houston Methodist and began his healthcare facilities career in 1990, giving him one of the longest continuous views of how energy management in hospitals has evolved from pneumatic controls to digital systems to AI-assisted platforms. His interview is notable for two reasons: he provided the most comprehensive historical narrative of the evolution of hospital energy management, and he is an active user of a third-party energy analytics platform (Energy Print) — making his observations on where current tools fall short directly actionable for product development.

"Their forecasting tool is a little bit weaker. I know there's better opportunities, especially with AI integration, better AI models that'll help with forecasting. And where we struggle is we're a system that grows a lot."
"That is unheard of nowadays. It's a meter issue is what I think. I just don't see the equipment we have in that building operating that poorly because of the newer equipment in there."
Recommendation 1: Study Energy Print as a Named Competitor in Houston / Texas Healthcare
Energy Print is Houston Methodist's current third-party energy analytics platform. Understanding its capabilities, pricing, and customer relationships in the Texas healthcare market is essential groundwork for any sales motion in this segment. Sean described specific gaps in forecasting and growth modeling — those gaps are the competitive opening.
Recommendation 2: Build Growth Modeling as a Forecasting Feature for Healthcare
Sean's clearest product gap is energy forecasting for a system that grows aggressively. 'What does our utility spend look like when we open a new 200,000 sq ft tower?' is a question no current platform can answer well. A scenario-based energy budget forecasting tool — one that accepts new building parameters and projects future utility spend — would be a direct response to a need Sean articulated clearly.
Recommendation 3: Confirm and Develop Power Factor Billing Detection
Sean is actively investigating what he suspects is a power factor meter malfunction at one of his campuses. Don Johnson identified power factor monitoring as his first unprompted feature request. If Clear Current's tariff analysis can flag power factor anomalies from billing data — not just alert on high charges but identify when the power factor reading itself looks metered incorrectly — that is a validated, healthcare-specific alert type.
Recommendation 4: Frame as a Billing Verification Layer on Top of Building Automation
Sean's hospital already has sophisticated BAS and demand management in place. The pitch here is not 'improve your energy operations' — it's 'verify that your utility is charging you correctly for the operations you're already managing.' The BAS optimizes; Clear Current confirms the bill reflects what the BAS actually did.

═══ SOURCE: Clear_Current_Interview_Summary_Texas_State.docx | FOLDER: Interview Summary Documents ═══
Interview Summary
Texas State University | Utility Operations
Andee Chamberlain, Carl Teague & James Norton
Conducted by Clayton Hester  |  March 2026
1. Interviewee Profiles
Primary: Andee Chamberlain
Supporting: Carl Teague
Supporting: James Norton
2. Texas State University - Energy Operations Overview
2.1 Infrastructure at a Glance
~98 buildings served by the utility operations team
~1,500 meters across campus - a mix of utility meters and internal sub-meters
District energy model: Central plants produce steam and chilled water, then distribute to buildings via a campus-wide system.
Self-supplied water: Texas State operates its own water wells, though drought restrictions are increasingly constraining allocations.
Mixed billing structure: A few large meters from the City of San Marcos for electricity, plus hundreds of internal sub-meters, plus individual city bills for some satellite buildings.
2.2 Energy Procurement
Electricity: Purchased from the City of San Marcos, a municipal utility. Not in a deregulated area, so no provider choice.
Natural gas: Purchased through an energy broker. They buy 'strips' (forward contracts) or go with market rate. Typically on 3-year terms.
Water: Self-supplied via wells, but drought restrictions are tightening. Wastewater is billed by the city based on water consumption.
2.3 Current Software
Texas State uses EnergyCAP as their primary energy management system. Andee described it as the platform where billing information and usage data are uploaded, historical data is stored, and projections are generated. However, they do not currently use EnergyCAP's bill processing module - their long-tenured bill processing employee recently left after 10-15 years, and they are considering outsourcing that function.
3. Key Interview Findings
3.1 EnergyCAP Is the Incumbent - But Has Gaps
When presented with the concept of an AI-powered energy management platform, Andee's first reaction was that it sounded similar to what EnergyCAP already provides - a critical competitive intelligence signal.
Andee went on to describe several areas where EnergyCAP falls short - particularly around district energy metering, blending internal sub-meter data with utility data, and creating billing from combined sources.
Implication for Clear Current: EnergyCAP is the competitor to beat in higher education. But the gap between what EnergyCAP promises and what it delivers on complex metering and district energy is a real wedge for Clear Current.
3.2 District Energy Metering Is an Underserved Problem
This was the richest and most specific pain point in the interview. Andee described the challenge of metering buildings that receive steam and chilled water from central plants rather than having their own equipment.
Implication for Clear Current: District energy systems represent a specific, high-complexity segment that current tools struggle with. If Clear Current can ingest and normalize data from both utility meters and internal sub-meters - and flag when data drops fail or meters go offline - that solves a problem Andee described as constant and manual.
3.3 The Data Tension Between Utility Ops and Finance
Implication for Clear Current: A platform that serves both the energy expert (demand, usage, conservation insights) and the finance user (cost, variance, budget tracking) from the same data set resolves a real internal tension. The AI chat interface could be particularly powerful - an energy manager and a CFO could ask different questions and get relevant answers from the same underlying data.
3.4 Bills Are Complex and Errors Are Real
With approximately 1,500 meters, Texas State's billing environment is sprawling. Andee confirmed that auditing bills is complex and time-intensive, and that errors do get found.
Implication for Clear Current: This validates the 'Spot Errors' capability as a lead use case. The combination of complexity (1,500 meters), human turnover (the bill processor leaving), and known error patterns creates a strong argument for an AI layer that catches what manual review misses.
3.5 Cost Recovery Model Limits Conservation Efforts
Texas State currently charges departments for building energy use based on square footage rather than actual consumption - a barrier to driving conservation behavior.
Implication for Clear Current: Better sub-metering data and building-level usage intelligence doesn't just serve the energy team - it enables a fundamentally different cost allocation model. The ROI story becomes: better data enables usage-based billing, which drives conservation, which reduces overall spend.
4. How They Would Use an AI Energy Management Tool
4.1 Andee Chamberlain - Conservation & Efficiency Focus
"Who's my biggest user?" Identify which buildings consume the most energy to prioritize conservation efforts
Energy Utilization Index (EUI) Square footage vs. energy consumption analysis at the building level
Energy per person Occupancy-normalized usage to understand where efficiency investments have the highest impact
4.2 Carl Teague - Event Impact & Demand Preparation
Event-correlated energy analysis "If somebody had an event at our university event center on a Saturday, how did that impact our consumption?"
Predictive event planning If events repeat annually and attendance is estimated, the system could project energy impact and help the team prepare
4.3 James Norton - Anomaly Detection & Alerting
Real-time anomaly flagging Immediate alerts when a building's gas, water, or electrical usage spikes unexpectedly
Root cause indicators A spike could mean a leak, unauthorized space use change, or equipment failure
5. Annual Energy Management Calendar - Texas State
6. Additional Insights
6.1 Water Is an Emerging Parallel Pain Point
Andee flagged water as a utility that is becoming increasingly complex and expensive. Texas State has its own wells, but drought restrictions are tightening allocations. This suggests that any energy management platform serving this market should consider water as part of the broader utility management picture.
6.2 Universities and Hospitals Share the Same Infrastructure Model
Andee explicitly linked universities and hospitals as the two primary institutional types that use district energy systems. This confirms the interview intelligence from Don Johnson at Trane, who also noted that hospitals are a key customer type with similar operational challenges.
6.3 Interest in Seeing the Research
Andee asked to see the final research output from this project, indicating genuine interest in the topic area and a willingness to engage further. This is a warm contact for future conversations about product development or pilot testing.
7. Strategic Recommendations for Dan & John
Prepared by Clayton Hester  |  CDL MBA Consulting Team
Team: Clayton Hester, Adam Case, Jason Wisnieski, Victor Pan
Clear Current Technologies  |  Dan Schreiber (CPO) & John Reuter (CEO)
Why This Interview Matters
Texas State University operates a district energy system serving approximately 98 buildings across campus, with roughly 1,500 meters and a mix of self-generated utilities (chilled water, steam, well water) and externally purchased electricity and gas. This interview provides a direct look at how a large, complex institutional energy buyer manages procurement, billing, and conservation - and where the gaps in current tooling create real operational pain. Critically, this team is an active EnergyCAP user, making their feedback a direct competitive intelligence source.

"It sounds kind of like what we already have. It sounds like a similar service to what EnergyCAP provides. But we don't use the bill processing software right now."
"EnergyCAP tells us that they can do that stuff, but it's harder than it sounds."
"Metering is really hard. Especially in district energy systems like most universities have. Hospitals and universities are kind of typically district energy places. And it's really hard to meter, to sub-meter, all of that stuff."
"It's not like once you install a meter and program it, it's always going to be perfect. That's just not the reality. The operations change. Maybe it wasn't installed correctly, maybe the data drop fails. It's just a constant, like trying to keep up with, you know, did we get that data?"
"One of my issues is being on the utility side. I care more about the demand and the information about our usage, but the bill processing folks care more about the bottom line numbers, like how much did it cost and how much did it cost last month? And so we're competing about what kind of data we get from that."
"It's really complex. Things change. And then our system of gathering that data and putting it into our database is also a little wonky."
"We had a weird multiplier happen in one of our bills. That's why you keep auditing your bills so that you can go, okay, why is this off? This is really weird. We've been spending 3,500 gallons every month, and now it's 35,000. Something's wrong."
"If we had better information about usage in our buildings, we can charge based on usage. And so that's a cost recovery thing that we could shift to. What happens from that is you can encourage efficiency or conservation. It's hard to drive that if you're charging people based on square footage."
"Those kinds of questions so that then we know where to focus on our energy conservation efforts. Where are we going to put our attention in a building and making it more efficient? Because that's our biggest bang for our buck."
"If I knew, if I had event schedules and know that this stuff is coming up, we could prepare for it. If the music department's gonna have a concert and we're gonna have estimated X amount of people on campus, that would give me a huge amount of information on how to prep for it."
"If we're typically using X amount of energy at a certain building and then it suddenly increases, that would be the biggest thing we'd want to know about right away. It could mean there's a leak or maybe the space is being used differently."
Period
Activity
Jan - Feb
Budget planning for next fiscal year begins. Administration assembles requests. Utility analyst pulls 3-5 years of historical data to establish trends.
Mar - May
Budget requests submitted, reviewed, approved or denied. Gas purchasing decisions evaluated (strips vs. market rate). 3-year forward planning.
Jun - Aug
Peak summer demand. Emergency generator testing (load bank at 80% capacity). End-of-fiscal-year budget reconciliation.
Sep 1
New fiscal year begins. Approved budgets take effect. New building projects may come online.
Sep - Dec
Monthly usage monitoring and reporting to management. Ongoing meter data quality management. Conservation project execution.
Ongoing
Monthly: usage and cost reporting to management. Continuous: bill auditing, meter data validation, anomaly investigation. Multi-year: gas purchasing strategy, infrastructure planning (3-5 year horizon).
Recommendation 1: Build a Competitive Positioning Against EnergyCAP
EnergyCAP is the default platform for university energy managers and was Andee's immediate frame of reference. Clear Current's messaging for this segment needs to directly address: 'Here's what we do that EnergyCAP cannot' - specifically around AI-driven analysis, district energy data blending, and intuitive chat-based querying.
Recommendation 2: Prioritize the District Energy / Sub-Metering Use Case
The inability to cleanly blend internal sub-meter data with utility meter data is a specific, painful, and unresolved problem for Texas State - and by extension, for most universities and hospitals with district energy systems. If Clear Current can ingest and normalize data from mixed meter sources, that is a defensible competitive advantage.
Recommendation 3: Design the Chat Interface to Serve Dual Audiences
The data tension between energy ops (demand, usage, conservation) and finance (cost, budget, variance) is real. Clear Current's AI chat interface should be designed so that both audiences can query the same dataset and get role-appropriate answers.
Recommendation 4: Consider Event-Correlated Energy Analysis as a Feature
Carl's desire to correlate campus events with energy consumption spikes is a specific, practical feature request that would resonate across universities, convention centers, stadiums, and hospitality.
Recommendation 5: Target the Bill Processing Transition Moment
Texas State's bill processing employee left after 10-15 years, creating an immediate operational gap. This is a repeatable pattern: long-tenured institutional knowledge walks out the door, and the organization scrambles. Clear Current's pitch for this segment should include: 'When your bill processing person leaves, we're the system that doesn't forget.'
