═══ SOURCE: Clear_Current_Interview_Summary_Troy_Nevells_UT_Austin (1).docx | FOLDER: Interview Summary Documents ═══
Interview Summary
Troy Nevells | UT Austin — Utilities & Energy Management
Director of Engineering & Technical Services
Conducted by Clayton Hester  |  April 14, 2026
1. Interviewee Profile
Troy is an engineer by training and career orientation. His perspective on energy management is production-side: how utilities are generated, distributed, and optimized within a centralized plant — not how bills are received, processed, and audited. His prior role at Yale included bill reconciliation, which gives him a practical reference point for the billing intelligence use case, even though UT Austin's district energy model means external utility invoices are relatively limited.
2. UT Austin — Energy Infrastructure Overview
UT Austin's energy system is substantially more self-sufficient than any other institution interviewed in this research program, including Texas State, BSMH, and the hospital systems. Understanding its structure is essential context for interpreting Troy's perspective.
Combined cycle production: Two gas turbines produce electricity by burning natural gas. Waste heat from those turbines feeds a heat recovery steam generator (HRSG), producing 'free' steam. That steam drives additional steam turbines, generating more electricity. Steam is also extracted mid-turbine for campus distribution at 165 PSI.
Generation capacity: UT produces approximately 100% of its own electricity on the main campus via this combined cycle process, with ~60 MW total capacity. Peak load is ~40 MW this time of year, rising to the summer peak when electrical chillers are running.
Resiliency and islanding: UT can island the campus — disconnect from the Austin Energy grid and run entirely on its own generation. This is a core operating capability.
Chill water system: 60,000 tons of chilled water generation capacity; peak load ~35,000 tons. The chill water system is the largest internal energy consumer on campus. Third-party optimizer: Optimum (used for about 10 years).
External utility relationships: Austin Energy provides a grid interconnect and charges a capacity charge for standby availability. UT's natural gas bills come from Texas Gas Service or equivalent. Monthly reconciliation is done between UT's internal meters and the utility provider meters.
3. Key Interview Findings
3.1 District Energy Systems: Different Use Case for AI Billing Intelligence
Troy was candid and direct: UT Austin's structure — producing nearly all of its own energy — means an AI billing intelligence platform focused on utility invoice analysis would have limited direct applicability in the short term. His primary billing touchpoints are natural gas invoices and electric import charges, not the tens of thousands of utility bills that multi-site commercial operators deal with.
He immediately identified who would benefit most: manufacturing companies with multiple electric meters per facility, large retail chains, quick-service restaurant operators. He specifically referenced Panda Restaurants by name as a prototypical use case — and mentioned Frito-Lay from his own prior experience, noting that supporting all of Frito-Lay North America with multiple meters per plant was a context where billing intelligence would have been directly valuable.
Implication for Clear Current: Troy's response is the clearest articulation in this research program of the market segmentation by customer type. District energy producers (large universities, hospital campuses that generate their own utilities) are secondary customers at best. The primary market is the utility purchaser with many meters across many locations — the QSR operator, the retailer, the manufacturer, the multi-site healthcare system that buys rather than produces energy.
3.2 Yale Experience: Hands-On Bill Reconciliation
Troy's prior role at Yale gives him direct first-person experience with the billing review process that Clear Current is designed to automate. At Yale, he was personally responsible for scrubbing monthly natural gas bills and electric import bills, and for reconciling UT's (or Yale's) internal production meters against the utility provider's meters.
He confirmed this reconciliation work is ongoing at UT Austin: the operations team compares utility provider meters to internal UEM meters monthly, looking for discrepancies.
Implication for Clear Current: This meter reconciliation workflow — utility meter vs. internal meter, side by side, monthly — is a specific, documented process that Clear Current's platform could automate or accelerate. For district energy producers who do purchase some external electricity or natural gas, the reconciliation audit is the relevant use case, not broad multi-meter anomaly detection. Clear Current should have a clear articulation of how the platform handles dual-meter environments.
3.3 Where Troy Does See AI Value — Smart Grid and Demand Response
Troy's most engaged and expansive response was around grid complexity: the challenge of managing multiple generation technologies (solar, wind, natural gas, battery storage, nuclear) on a single grid, all with different variability profiles. He described this as the space where AI has the clearest near-term value — not in billing, but in grid management and demand response.
He also described UT Austin's existing demand response capability: when generation assets trip, the campus can automatically shed load from non-critical buildings to protect critical systems. However, this is currently done in large, crude chunks — shutting off entire buildings rather than specific loads. He envisioned AI enabling granular, prioritized load shedding: shutting off air conditioning in residential dorms before research labs, or individual equipment within buildings before entire floors.
Implication for Clear Current: This demand response optimization use case is outside Clear Current's current scope but is a genuinely valuable insight for product roadmap conversations with John and Dan. The ability to interface with building automation systems to enable intelligent load shedding during grid stress events is a capability that large district energy producers would find compelling. It is also adjacent to the regulatory intelligence feature Phil Combs described — if Clear Current monitors ERCOT dispatch signals alongside billing data, it could alert university energy teams to upcoming grid events that will affect their production decisions.
3.4 Weather-Correlated Forecasting — A Neighboring UT Student Project
Troy mentioned in passing that UT Austin is already working with a student-formed company ('Texas Energy Consultants') that is building an AI model to forecast campus energy load one week in advance, using weather forecasts as the primary input variable. This project is presenting its findings the following week.
He connected this directly to the billing intelligence concept: if a platform could integrate weather-correlated load forecasting with billing data, it could distinguish between anomalies caused by weather events versus equipment failures versus billing errors — making the anomaly detection far more precise.
Implication for Clear Current: This weather-correlated anomaly detection is a feature Phil Combs also described from the M&V engineering perspective: isolating the weather variable to understand whether energy changes are driven by climate, operations, or billing. It is not a current Clear Current capability but has now been validated by two different types of interviewees — an M&V engineer and a district energy director — as a meaningful upgrade to basic anomaly detection.
3.5 Carbon Taxation and Regulatory Complexity as a Growing Factor
Troy raised carbon taxation in the context of northeastern states (New York, Massachusetts) that have instituted carbon pricing linked to natural gas consumption. He noted that for customers subject to these regimes, the billing complexity increases significantly — carbon charges are now tied to natural gas billing, adding another layer of potential error and another type of tariff that requires interpretation.
Implication for Clear Current: This is an early-stage regulatory intelligence use case that Phil Combs and Andi Ault also touched on from different angles. Carbon-linked billing surcharges in regulated states are complex, variable, and not well-understood by most customers. If Clear Current's tariff engine can identify and explain carbon charge components on utility bills, that is a differentiated capability for customers in northeastern states — and will become increasingly relevant as carbon pricing expands.
4. Strategic Recommendations for Dan & John
Prepared by Clayton Hester  |  CDL MBA Consulting Team
Team: Clayton Hester, Adam Case, Jason Wisnieski, Victor Pan
Clear Current Technologies  |  Dan Schreiber (CPO) & John Reuter (CEO)
Why This Interview Matters
Troy Nevells is the Director of Engineering and Technical Services within UT Austin's Utilities & Energy Management (UEM) department, a role he has held for only seven months after joining from Yale University's power plant operations. He represents the engineering and operations complement to Andi Ault's finance-side interview earlier in this research program — together, they provide a complete dual-audience view of how UT Austin's UEM division manages one of the most complex campus energy systems in the country. Troy's interview is distinguished by his power generation engineering background (gas turbines, steam turbines, combined-cycle systems) and his perspective on where AI-powered energy management has genuine near-term applicability for district energy producers, as opposed to the utility purchasers who are Clear Current's primary target.

Interview Method
Live call with Clayton Hester  |  April 14, 2026
"Yeah, I mean, if the University of Texas didn't have a district energy system and they had 25 buildings each with a utility meter, a water meter, an electrical meter, a natural gas meter — I can imagine that something like this would be very beneficial."
"I was responsible there to scrub through the monthly natural gas bills and the monthly electric bills... we compare our natural gas bills compared to Texas gas billing, and then we're comparing Austin Energy's bills to our internal UEM electric meters as well."
"If you had all these mixes of technologies between solar and wind and battery and natural gas or other type technologies — that managing your assets and the variability in the assets, I can imagine AI would definitely help on that front."
"They're using AI and they are trying to help us forecast using the outside temperatures and humidities and trying to help us forecast like one week in advance what our load should be based on a forecasted outlook on temperature."
Recommendation 1: Use UT Austin as a District Energy Reference Case — Not a Target Customer
Troy's analysis was clear and honest: UT Austin is not the primary customer for an AI billing intelligence platform. The district energy structure limits the external bill volume. However, UT Austin's articulation of who would benefit — multi-site purchasers like Panda, large manufacturers like Frito-Lay, retailers — is a strong endorsement of Clear Current's target market from a credible institutional source. That reference is valuable even if UT Austin is not a direct buyer.
Recommendation 2: Build a Meter Reconciliation Feature for District Energy Producers
Even self-generating institutions like UT Austin have external utility bills — natural gas, standby electric, water. Troy described doing monthly reconciliation between utility meters and internal production meters manually. A platform that automates this reconciliation and flags discrepancies would be useful even for district energy producers, and represents a low-complexity, high-credibility entry point into the university and hospital campus market.
Recommendation 3: Consider Weather-Correlated Anomaly Detection as a Product Roadmap Item
Troy described UT Austin's student project building weather-to-load correlation forecasting. Phil Combs described regression against weather as the core methodology in M&V engineering. Integrating weather correlation into Clear Current's anomaly detection — so the system can distinguish weather-driven consumption changes from billing errors or equipment issues — would move the product from basic spike detection to statistically meaningful anomaly identification. This is a meaningful product upgrade backed by multiple expert perspectives.
Recommendation 4: Monitor Carbon Charge Complexity as a Future Regulatory Feature
Troy flagged carbon taxation linked to natural gas billing in northeastern states as an emerging billing complexity layer. As carbon pricing expands, customers who cannot interpret their own carbon charge components will need a platform that can explain them. Clear Current's tariff analysis should be designed to identify and explain these charges when they appear, positioning the platform as a regulatory intelligence resource beyond just error detection.

═══ SOURCE: Clear_Current_Interview_Summary_Walt_Taylor_Panda.docx | FOLDER: Interview Summary Documents ═══
Interview Summary
Walt Taylor  |  Panda Restaurant Group
Energy Manager (Contractor)
Conducted by Clayton Hester  |  April 16, 2026
1. Interviewee Profile
Walt’s interview classification as “Contractor” in the Otter.ai transcript is consistent with his background: he runs his own energy consulting firm, GWT2Energy, and engages with Panda in an advisory capacity rather than as a corporate employee. This gives him unusual candor relative to in-house staff, and his perspective reflects both the Panda operational reality and the broader QSR energy landscape he serves through GWT2Energy.
He is one of the few interviewees in this research program with hands-on experience getting a utility rate changed through a state Public Service Commission, which he accomplished by making a quantitative case that the utility’s calculation methodology was inequitable.
2. Panda Restaurant Group — Energy Operations Overview
2.1 Current System Architecture
Panda manages energy across approximately 2,300 locations using a hybrid model:
ENGIE Impact (referred to as “NG” throughout the interview) reviews all utility bills and flags outliers in usage or cost. Panda does not review bills line by line themselves.
Energy Management Systems (EMS) are actively being rolled out across the portfolio to remotely monitor and control HVAC thermostats, parking lot lighting, and exterior lighting.
Water sensors have been deployed across most or all locations to monitor usage and generate alerts.
Management is by exception: the team looks at averages, flags the top 20% of outliers, and investigates those. They are not trying to optimize every location individually.
No AI is currently in use anywhere in Panda’s energy management process.
2.2 EMS Rollout — What It Does and Why
The energy management system currently being deployed performs the following:
Thermostat control: removes set-point authority from individual operators and enforces minimum thresholds. Operators previously set thermostats to 65°F in summer, which was a major waste source.
Lighting control: manages parking lot and exterior lighting based on time schedules rather than photo sensors, which fail or get dirty and leave lights on 24/7.
Electricity usage and demand monitoring: tracks total consumption and demand peaks at each location to help manage peak demand pricing.
Walt noted pushback from operations teams who want colder dining rooms. He described this as a permanent balancing act between energy savings and staff retention: if the restaurant is too warm, employees walk out.
2.3 Seasonal Calendar
Panda’s energy management calendar is simple and unseasonal:
Budgeting: October, for a January–December fiscal year.
Outlier monitoring: daily, ongoing. Not concentrated in any particular season.
The most seasonally sensitive variable is peak demand, which shifts with climate region and time-of-use rate structures.
3. The ENGIE Impact / Arcadia Relationship — Confirmed
This is the single most commercially significant finding in the interview. Walt confirmed unprompted that ENGIE Impact uses Arcadia to aggregate electronic utility bill data, and separately uses OCR for paper bills:
Walt also provided historical context: the predecessor platform to Arcadia at ENGIE was a company called Urgenet, which was later acquired by Arcadia. ENGIE then made a deliberate decision to stop developing its own bill aggregation software and rely on Arcadia’s platform instead.
3.1 Independent Confirmation from Arcadia’s Own Documentation
This finding was confirmed through Arcadia’s published technical documentation. Arcadia’s developer docs explicitly address the ENGIE Insight platform as a known indirect data source in their integration architecture — meaning Arcadia has built a specific pathway to ingest data through ENGIE Insight when customers grant access to the ENGIE portal rather than directly to their utility accounts. Arcadia designates these as “INDIRECT” sources and notes that direct utility credentials are always preferred.
3.2 Implications for Clear Current
4. Utility Rate Complexity — The Real GTM Barrier
Walt spent more time on rate complexity than any topic except the ENGIE/Arcadia finding. His perspective is the most technically detailed of any interviewee in this program.
4.1 The Heterogeneity Problem
Alabama Power alone has approximately 45 rate structures. An AI tool that checks every rate at every utility for every location would be checking thousands of permutations at scale — and most of them are irrelevant because the customer is already on the best available rate.
Electricity, water, sewer, and natural gas each have different structures. Natural gas is relatively simple (straight usage). Electricity and water are complex. Sewer is the most structurally unfair.
No two restaurant locations bill identically, even when adjacent, because of building age, orientation, square footage, ceiling height, equipment vintage, and operator behavior.
4.2 The Ratchet Rate (Georgia Power Example)
Walt gave one of the most detailed rate structure explanations in the research program, using Georgia Power as an example. Georgia Power does not charge separately for peak demand — it is embedded in a ratchet mechanism:
Whatever a customer’s peak demand is during the hottest month of the year (e.g., June), that peak becomes the basis for their capacity charge.
That charge is applied for the following nine months at 100%, and in the remaining three winter months at 90% of the peak.
If conditions change, the utility adjusts to whatever is most advantageous for them. Walt noted this is not necessarily unfair in principle, but the calculation is opaque and embedded in fine print.
4.3 Sewer Overbilling — An Uncontested Structural Issue
This is a new finding not previously surfaced in the research program. Walt described a systematic billing error category that is legally permitted but structurally incorrect:
Water utilities charge sewer rates based on water intake, on the assumption that most water entering a building is also discharged as sewage.
This assumption is valid for office buildings and homes. It is wrong for restaurants. At Panda Express, the two largest water uses are ice and drinks — both of which are consumed offsite by customers.
There is no meter tracking how much water actually leaves a restaurant as sewage. The utility simply charges as if all input water becomes sewage.
West Coast markets are particularly exposed because water and sewer remediation costs are among the highest in the country.
Walt acknowledged there is currently no way to prove the actual sewage discharge amount. This is a structural issue, not an invoicing error — but it represents a category of systematic overbilling that nobody in the industry is currently contesting.
4.4 Peak Demand and the California Duck Curve
Walt introduced the California duck curve problem in the context of time-of-use rate structures:
Historically, peak demand in California occurred mid-afternoon, similar to Texas. Utilities structured time-of-use rates accordingly.
As California pushed residential solar adoption, solar production now floods the grid during mid-day. Peak demand has shifted to evenings when solar drops off.
Utilities have adjusted their rate structures to follow the new peak, meaning companies that positioned their operations around old peak windows now face new exposures.
Walt’s framing: utilities will always adjust their rate structures to remain profitable. Energy managers who track only current rates will continually be caught behind.
5. Clear Current Fit Assessment
5.1 Where the Tool Works (Walt’s Assessment)
Walt was candid that the AI billing tool concept is more credible for smaller multi-site operators than for Panda at its current scale:
A franchisee with 25 restaurants and one or two utilities: “I think that probably works.” Fewer utilities, more likely electronic bill availability, lower rate complexity.
The tool’s document-chat capability would be genuinely useful for answering operator questions: why is this Philadelphia store’s bill so much higher than that one? These questions currently require manual bill downloads and cross-referencing.
Rate tariff analysis, if done correctly, would surface savings that ENGIE doesn’t catch because ENGIE assumes most customers are already on optimal rates.
5.2 Where Walt Is Skeptical
At Panda’s scale, the complexity is not just bill volume — it’s the combination of thousands of rate structures, paper bill OCR gaps, and location-level variability that resists standardization.
Energy is not like writing code: “Writing code does the same thing every time and you get the same result every time. That’s not how it is when you’re looking at utility bills.”
He would need to see demonstrated accuracy at scale before trusting an AI system enough to replace manual review. He expressed this as a confidence gap, not a technology rejection.
Walt also noted that restaurants are manufacturing facilities inside 2,000-square-foot boxes. Vendors who claim they can solve restaurant energy without understanding the operational environment — hoods, kitchen equipment, pressure balancing — consistently fail. He has seen this pattern repeatedly.
5.3 Questions Walt Would Ask an LLM-Powered ENGIE
When asked what he would do if ENGIE had an LLM interface, Walt provided the clearest picture of what an enterprise energy manager actually needs:
Why are my 20 stores in Philadelphia billing so differently? Break down rates, usage patterns, and peak charges across utilities.
What are our top outliers this month and what is driving them — not just that they’re outliers, but why?
Flag when a specific meter or piece of equipment drove a usage spike that will trigger a ratchet rate for the next nine months.
A conversation interface that could replace manually downloading bills, cross-referencing, and producing a written explanation for an operator.
6. Bonus Insight — Square Footage Is the Wrong Metric
Walt offered an unprompted critique of the foundational energy benchmarking standard:
The industry benchmarks energy performance as usage per square foot. This is incorrect. Energy is used to heat and cool cubic volume, not floor area.
A hotel lobby and an office floor of identical square footage have entirely different energy profiles because the hotel lobby is a large open volume.
The reason the industry uses square footage is that it’s how real estate is sold — not because it’s the right unit. Walt called this “laziness.”
Implication for Clear Current: if the platform’s benchmarking or peer comparison features use standard square footage metrics, they will inherit this industry flaw. Flagging ceiling height or building type as a normalization variable would differentiate the product from every existing tool.
7. Key Quotes
Confidential | MBA Research Engagement — Texas A&M Mays Business School
CDL-Texas Energy Stream | Clear Current Technologies | Clayton Hester, Jason Wisnieski, Adam Case, Victor Pan
Why This Interview Matters
Walt Taylor is one of the most operationally grounded voices in this entire research program. As the energy manager overseeing approximately 2,300 Panda Restaurant Group locations, he sits at the intersection of large-scale multi-site bill management, EMS technology rollout, and hands-on utility dispute experience. He is a 25-year restaurant energy veteran who has worked both the in-house client side (at Panda and other QSR brands) and the consulting side through his own firm, GWT2Energy. This interview produced three findings that no other interview surfaced: (1) direct confirmation that ENGIE Impact uses Arcadia to aggregate electronic utility bill data, (2) a detailed articulation of why AI billing tools struggle at enterprise scale and where they do work, and (3) a new structural overbilling category — sewer charges based on water intake rather than actual discharge — that is systematic, untracked, and effectively un-contestable under current utility norms.
⚠  COMPETITIVE INTELLIGENCE ALERT — READ FIRST
Walt Taylor confirmed on the record that ENGIE Impact uses Arcadia to aggregate electronic utility bill data, and relies on OCR for paper bills. This was not in our prior research design. It was independently confirmed by Arcadia’s own technical documentation, which explicitly treats ENGIE Insight as a known third-party indirect data source in its integration architecture. This means: (1) ENGIE’s data backbone is Arcadia, not proprietary, (2) ENGIE’s paper bill coverage remains OCR-dependent, and (3) the most direct route to undercutting ENGIE is to go directly to Arcadia’s data layer — or to outperform OCR on paper bills. Walt’s unprompted observation that ENGIE should simply “bolt on” a large language model rather than allow a competitor to do it is equally important: he is telling us that the threat is visible from inside the customer base.

“NG actually uses Arcadia to get their electronic bills and then they get the paper bills, you know, mailed to them and they run through the OCR.”
— Walt Taylor, on ENGIE Impact’s data architecture
Implication
Detail
ENGIE’s data backbone is not proprietary
ENGIE Impact built its bill aggregation capability on top of Arcadia, not from scratch. This means ENGIE’s data quality ceiling is Arcadia’s data quality ceiling.
Paper bills remain OCR-dependent
Even with Arcadia for electronic bills, ENGIE still relies on OCR for paper bills. Walt confirmed OCR doesn’t work 100% of the time and that catching 90% of errors is the realistic target.
The Arcadia layer is accessible
A competitor that integrates directly with Arcadia’s data layer could potentially match ENGIE’s electronic bill coverage without building its own aggregation infrastructure.
The LLM gap is visible to customers
Walt explicitly said ENGIE should just “bolt on” an LLM since they already have all the data. He expressed this as a natural next step — and his willingness to work with Claude himself shows low friction for AI adoption.
“NG actually uses Arcadia to get their electronic bills and then they get the paper bills mailed to them and they run through the OCR.”
— On ENGIE Impact’s data architecture
“Even at that point, their OCR recognition doesn’t work 100% of the time. You’re never going to catch every mistake. So what we shoot to do is try to catch 90% of them.”
— On the limitations of ENGIE’s bill processing
“It would be much better if, since NG already houses all that data, they would just work with Anthropic or whoever and bolt you on.”
— On why ENGIE adding an LLM is the obvious next move
“Writing code does the same thing every time and you get the same result every time. That’s not how it is when you’re looking at utility bills and usage and all the different things.”
— On why energy AI is harder than it looks
“Square footage has nothing to do with how much energy. It’s your cubic footage that has everything to do with how much energy is used, because you don’t heat a space by the floor. You heat a space like a cube.”
— On the fundamental flaw in energy benchmarking
“A lot of times, utilities just believe they’re right about everything and don’t want to talk to you about it.”
— On the challenge of disputing billing errors with utilities
